stem: During an abdominal surgery, an artery located posterior to the epiploic (Winslow's) foramen is accidentally injured. Which major vessel is most likely involved?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
  • The epiploic (Winslow's) foramen connects the greater sac and lesser sac.
  • Its posterior boundary is formed by the inferior vena cava (IVC) covered by peritoneum.
  • Therefore, a vessel injured posterior to the foramen most likely involves the IVC.
  • The anterior boundary is the portal triad-portal vein, hepatic artery proper, and common bile duct-within the hepatoduodenal ligament.
  • Persistent bleeding despite a Pringle maneuver (clamping the hepatoduodenal ligament) suggests injury to the IVC or hepatic veins rather than the portal triad.


Other Options:
Option B: The abdominal aorta lies deeper in the retroperitoneum and is not a boundary of the foramen.
Option C: The portal vein is the anterior boundary, not posterior.
Option D: The hepatic artery lies in the anterior boundary within the hepatoduodenal ligament.

stem: Which cranial nerves supply the anterior and posterior bellies of the digastric muscle respectively?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
  • The anterior belly of the digastric is supplied by the nerve to mylohyoid (branch of V3).
  • The posterior belly is supplied by the facial nerve (CN VII).
  • This dual innervation reflects their different pharyngeal arch origins (first and second).
  • Functionally, the digastric assists in depressing the mandible and elevating the hyoid bone.
  • Innervation follows arch development.


Other Options:
Option A: Incorrect; because the facial nerve supplies only the posterior belly.
Option C: Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX) supplies stylopharyngeus, not digastric.
Option D: V3 supplies only the anterior belly, not both.

stem: During a neck surgery, the submandibular gland along with its duct is exposed and removed. Intraoperatively, a nerve running beneath the gland is identified. Which nerve is this most likely to be?
options: Marginal mandibular branch of facial nerve
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
  • The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) runs beneath the submandibular gland and duct.
  • It supplies all intrinsic and extrinsic muscles of the tongue (except palatoglossus).
  • This relationship is important in submandibular gland surgeries.
  • Injury causes tongue deviation toward the side of the lesion due to genioglossus weakness.


Other Options:
Option A: The lingual nerve loops inferior to the duct but does not run beneath the gland.
Option B: The mylohyoid nerve supplies the anterior digastric and mylohyoid.
Option D: The marginal mandibular branch of CN VII supplies facial muscles of the lower lip.

stem: Which organ is this from?
subject: Histology
explanation:
Solution
  • The ureter is a muscular tube conveying urine from the kidney to the bladder.
  • It is lined by transitional epithelium (urothelium).
  • It typically shows a stellate (star-shaped) lumen due to muscle contraction.
  • Narrowing occurs at the PUJ, pelvic brim, and vesico-ureteric junction.
  • Stones commonly lodge at these constrictions.


Other Options:
Option A: Fallopian tube is lined by ciliated columnar epithelium.
Option C: Small intestine shows villi with simple columnar cells.
Option D: Gallbladder has tall columnar epithelium, no muscularis mucosa.

stem: All of the following cranial nerves pass through this foramen except:
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
  • The jugular foramen transmits CN IX, X, and XI.
  • It also allows passage of the internal jugular vein.
  • The hypoglossal nerve (CN XII) instead passes through the hypoglossal canal.
  • Lesions here cause Vernet's syndrome (IX, X, XI palsy).
  • CN XII involvement rules out jugular foramen pathology.


Other Options:
Option A: Glossopharyngeal nerve passes via jugular foramen.
Option B: Vagus nerve exits via jugular foramen.
Option C: Accessory nerve exits via jugular foramen.

stem: A 25-year-old athlete sustains a sports injury causing forced eversion of the foot. Which ligament is most likely injured?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation:
Solution
  • The deltoid ligament prevents excessive eversion at the ankle.
  • It is attached to the medial malleolus and spreads to the talus, calcaneus, and navicular.
  • Strong ligament → usually bone fractures (medial malleolus) before rupture.
  • Eversion injuries are less common but more severe.
  • Associated with Pott's fracture-dislocation.


Other Options:
Option A: Stabilizes tibiofibular syndesmosis, not primary restraint to eversion.
Option B: Resists inversion, not eversion.
Option C: Part of lateral ligament; injured in inversion sprains.

stem: Regarding cranial nerves IX and X, which fiber type is not carried?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
  • CN IX and CN X contain GSA fibers (ear, pharynx sensation).
  • They contain GVA fibers (carotid body, aortic body, thoracic/abdominal viscera).
  • They also carry GVE parasympathetic fibers (to glands/organs).
  • They also have SVE fibers (muscles of pharyngeal arches).
  • They lack GSE fibers, which are found in CN III, IV, VI, and XII.


Other Options:
Option A: GSA present (pharynx, ear sensation).
Option B: GVA present (visceral input).
Option D: GVE present (parasympathetic).

stem: A newborn has a patent ductus arteriosus (PDA). This vessel is derived from which embryological arch?
subject: Embryology
explanation:
Solution
  • The ductus arteriosus develops from the left sixth aortic arch.
  • Connects pulmonary trunk to descending aorta. After birth closes to form ligamentum arteriosum.
  • PDA causes left-to-right shunt → pulmonary hypertension.
  • Left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops around the ligamentum arteriosum.


Other Options:
Option A: Forms part of maxillary artery.
Option B: Forms common carotid and proximal internal carotid arteries.
Option C: Contributes to the arch of aorta (left) and right subclavian (right).

stem: A 10-year-old child is evaluated for enlarged and orange-colored tonsils. He has a history of a family member's death due to a cardiovascular abnormality. Laboratory investigations reveal very low HDL levels (5 mg/dL), triglycerides = 146 mg/dL, and total cholesterol = 80 mg/dL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • Tangier's disease is a rare autosomal recessive disorder due to ABCA1 transporter mutation, leading to defective cholesterol efflux.
  • This results in extremely low HDL levels, often <10 mg/dL.
  • Clinically associated with orange-colored tonsils, hepatosplenomegaly, and peripheral neuropathy.
  • Patients are at high risk of premature cardiovascular disease.


Other Options:
Option B: Abetalipoproteinemia → absence of apoB lipoproteins (chylomicrons, VLDL, LDL); causes fat malabsorption and acanthocytosis, not orange tonsils.
Option C: Niemann-Pick disease defective sphingomyelinase; causes lipid storage with hepatosplenomegaly and neurological decline, not isolated low HDL.
Option D: Incorrect; specific diagnosis is Tangier's disease.

stem: A 20-year-old boy presents with tendon xanthomas on the elbow. He has a strong family history of premature cardiovascular disease his father died of myocardial infarction at 35. Laboratory reports show total cholesterol = 470 mg/dL LDL = 300 mg/dL, and normal triglycerides. Which of the following types of hyperlipoproteinemia is most likely?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • Type IIa hyperlipoproteinemia (Familial hypercholesterolemia) is due to LDL receptor deficiency.
  • Characterized by markedly elevated LDL and total cholesterol with normal triglycerides.
  • Clinical features: xanthomas, premature atherosclerosis, early MI.
  • Autosomal dominant inheritance with family clustering.


Other Options:
Option A: Type I familial chylomicronemia; causes elevated TG, pancreatitis, eruptive xanthomas, not tendon xanthomas.
Option C: Type IIb elevated LDL + VLDL; both cholesterol and triglycerides rise.
Option D: Type III → due to apoE mutation causes accumulation of chylomicron remnants and IDL with palmar xanthomas.

stem: A patient has vitamin B12 deficiency due to ileal resection. On examination, he has a positive Romberg sign. Laboratory investigations reveal macrocytic anemia, elevated homocysteine, and elevated methylmalonic acid (MMA). Which of the following amino acids is likely to be deficient in this patient?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • Vitamin B12 is essential for methionine synthase activity (homocysteine to methionine).
  • In deficiency, methionine levels drop and homocysteine accumulates.
  • Also needed for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (MMA accumulates when deficient).
  • Neurological signs (positive Romberg) due to defective myelin synthesis.


Other Options:
Option A: Phenylalanine metabolism depends on BH4, not B12.
Option C: Cysteine formed from homocysteine requires vitamin B6 (PLP), not B12.
Option D: Glutamine metabolism unrelated to B12.

stem: A 6-month-old infant diagnosed with phenylketonuria (PKU) is started on a phenylalanine-restricted diet. Despite compliance, the child continues to have developmental delay, elevated phenylalanine, and decreased tyrosine. Genetic testing shows a normal phenylalanine hydroxylase gene. Which deficiency most likely explains this atypical presentation?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • Atypical PKU may result from deficiency of tetrahydrobiopterin (BH4), a cofactor for phenylalanine hydroxylase.
  • Without BH4, phenylalanine → tyrosine conversion fails despite normal enzyme.
  • This leads to high phenylalanine and low tyrosine with neurological features.
  • BH4 is also needed for synthesis of dopamine, serotonin, nitric oxide.


Other Options:
Option B: THFA (tetrahydrofolate) is required for 1-carbon metabolism, not phenylalanine hydroxylase.
Option C: PLP (B6) is a cofactor for transamination/decarboxylation, not BH4-dependent hydroxylation.
Option D: Adenosyl B12 is cofactor for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase, not related to PKU.

stem: A 9-month-old infant presents with developmental regression, hypotonia, and exaggerated startle response. Fundus examination shows a cherry-red spot. Enzyme assay reveals hexosaminidase A deficiency. Which substance accumulates?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • Tay-Sachs disease is caused by hexosaminidase A deficiency, leading to accumulation of GM2 gangliosides in neurons.
  • Clinical triad: neurodegeneration, developmental delay, and cherry-red spot on macula.
  • Importantly, there is no hepatosplenomegaly (which differentiates it from Niemann-Pick).


Other Options:
Option A: GM1 gangliosides accumulate in GM1 gangliosidosis.
Option C: Galactocerebroside accumulates in Krabbe disease.
Option D: Sphingolipids is too general; specific storage here is GM2.

stem: Phrynoderma with follicular hyperkeratosis is noted. Which of the following should be the next step?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Phrynoderma (toad skin) is caused by vitamin A deficiency.
  • Associated with follicular hyperkeratosis and rough skin.
  • The most important next step is an ocular exam to check for Bitot's spots, xerophthalmia and keratomalacia.
  • Early diagnosis prevents blindness.


Other Options:
Option B: Vitamin D deficiency causes rickets/osteomalacia, not phrynoderma.
Option C: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia, neuropathy.
Option D: DTRs not related to vitamin A deficiency.

stem: A 2-year-old child presents with developmental delay, coarse facial features, hepatosplenomegaly, and corneal clouding. Enzyme assay reveals deficiency of alpha-L-iduronidase. Which substance is expected to accumulate?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • Deficiency of alpha-L-iduronidase causes Hurler's syndrome (MPS I).
  • Leads to accumulation of dermatan sulfate and heparan sulfate (often grouped as dermatan + keratan sulfate in exams).
  • Clinical features: developmental delay, gargoyle facies, hepatosplenomegaly, corneal clouding. Inheritance is autosomal recessive.


Other Options:
Option A: Dermatan sulfate alone is incomplete; both dermatan + heparan (keratan) accumulate.
Option C: Keratan sulfate alone seen in Morquio syndrome (MPS IV).
Option D: Chondroitin sulfate not characteristic of MPS I.

stem: A 5-year-old malnourished child presents with bleeding gums, perifollicular hemorrhages, and poor wound healing. Which enzyme is most likely impaired?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • Vitamin C is required as a cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase in collagen synthesis.
  • Deficiency leads to scurvy, characterized by bleeding gums, impaired wound healing, perifollicular hemorrhage.
  • Hydroxylation of proline/lysine residues provides collagen stability via hydrogen bonding. Without vitamin C, defective collagen results in fragile vessels.
  • Classic clinical triad = bleeding, poor healing, swollen gums.


Other Options:
Option B: Lysyl oxidase requires copper, not vitamin C.
Option C: Galactosyl transferase participates in glycosylation, not collagen hydroxylation.
Option D: Glutathione peroxidase is selenium-dependent.

stem: An mRNA consists of a coding sequence of 900 nucleotides. A frameshift mutation occurs at the 4th nucleotide. What is the most likely outcome?
options: Partial loss of protein and function
Complete loss of protein and function
No change as corrected in post-translation
subject: Genetics
explanation:
Solution
  • A frameshift mutation alters the reading frame from the point of mutation.
  • Since it occurs at the 4th nucleotide, nearly the entire sequence downstream is affected.
  • This results in premature stop codons, truncated protein, and loss of function.
  • Frameshift mutations are more severe than point mutations.


Other Options:
Option A: Wrong; frameshift causes major disruption.
Option B: Severe frameshift at 4th base causes complete loss, not partial.
Option D: Post-translational modifications cannot fix frameshift errors.

stem: A patient has severe dyspnea. ABG reveals: pH 7.2, pCO2=66 mmHg, HCO3-=26 mmol/L. What is the underlying disorder?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • pH 7.2 = acidosis.
  • pCO2 elevated (66 mmHg) = respiratory cause.
  • Bicarbonate is near normal (26 mmol/L) = no metabolic compensation.
  • Thus, diagnosis = acute respiratory acidosis.
  • Common causes: airway obstruction, COPD exacerbation, hypoventilation.


Other Options:
Option A: Metabolic acidosis would show low HCO3- not present here.
Option B: Metabolic alkalosis presents with pH up and HCO3- up.
Option D: Respiratory alkalosis would have decreased pCO2.

stem: ABG values: pH 7.3, PCO2=44 mmHg, HCO3-=16 mmol/L, base excess = -12, Na+ 130 mmol/L, Cl- 84 mmol/L. Which explains the defect?
options: Partially compensated respiratory acidosis with AG 16.
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • This ABG demonstrates acidosis (pH 7.3) with low bicarbonate (16 mmol/L) and normal pCO2 (44 mmHg), confirming a metabolic cause.
  • Calculated anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 130 - (84 + 16) = 30, indicating a high anion gap metabolic acidosis.
  • Common causes include lactic acidosis, diabetic ketoacidosis, renal failure, and toxin ingestion (e.g., methanol, ethylene glycol).


Other Options:
Option A: Respiratory acidosis requires elevated pCO2, which is not present.
Option B: Chronic respiratory acidosis is incorrect because pCO2 is normal.
Option D: The anion gap is 30, not 16; hence this is not simple acute metabolic acidosis with normal AG.

stem: A man was trapped in a tunnel for 5 days. Which mechanism provides energy to the brain during this period?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • In prolonged starvation (>3 days), the brain shifts to using ketone bodies (acetoacetate and beta-hydroxybutyrate) as its primary energy source.
  • These are generated by the liver during hepatic ketogenesis.
  • This adaptation spares muscle protein breakdown, reducing reliance on gluconeogenesis.
  • Glycogen stores are exhausted within 24 hours, and fatty acids cannot be used directly by the brain due to the blood-brain barrier.


Other Options:
Option A: Gluconeogenesis continues but cannot fully meet the brain's energy demand during prolonged fasting.
Option B: Lipolysis provides fatty acids, but these cannot be used by the brain.
Option C: Glycogenolysis lasts only about 24 hours, so it is not a major energy source at day 5.

stem: A patient presents with right heart failure, nystagmus, and ataxia. He is nonalcoholic with no addictions. Which deficiency causes these symptoms?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Thiamine (Vitamin B1) deficiency causes beriberi and Wernicke's encephalopathy.
  • Wet beriberi → high-output cardiac failure (right-sided heart failure).
  • Dry beriberi → peripheral neuropathy.
  • Wernicke's encephalopathy consists of a triad: nystagmus, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia.
  • While most commonly seen in alcoholics, it can also occur in malnutrition, chronic illness, or post-GI surgery.


Other Options:
Option B: Zinc deficiency growth retardation, delayed wound healing, hypogonadism.
Option C: Selenium deficiency cardiomyopathy (Keshan disease) but not CNS signs.
Option D: Vitamin B12 deficiency → subacute combined degeneration, megaloblastic anemia, but not acute heart failure.

stem: If glucose level is increased 2-3 times above normal and maintained, what happens to Insulin?
options: Initial rise followed by another increase
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Insulin secretion is biphasic:
  • Phase 1: A rapid release of pre-formed insulin granules → sudden spike.
  • Phase 2: If hyperglycemia persists, insulin secretion falls to a sustained but lower level as granule stores are depleted.
  • This mechanism explains early insulin peaks in glucose tolerance testing and helps distinguish normal vs. diabetic physiology.


Other Options:
Option B: Not continuous; secretion declines after depletion.
Option C: Levels do not fall below baseline.
Option D: There is no second rise-only an initial sharp rise followed by a fall.

stem: In a renal physiology experiment, the following pressures were measured to assess glomerular filtration: Glomerular capillary hydrostatic pressure = 18 mmHg, Glomerular capillary oncotic pressure = 27 mmHg, Bowman's space oncotic pressure = 7 mmHg. Assuming Bowman's space hydrostatic pressure is negligible, what is the net filtration pressure (NFP) across the glomerular capillary wall?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Net Filtration Pressure (NFP) = (Glomerular hydrostatic + Bowman's space oncotic) - (Glomerular oncotic + Bowman's hydrostatic).
  • Calculation: (18 + 7) - (27 + 0) = 25 - 27 = -2 mmHg.
  • A negative NFP means filtration is opposed and reabsorption may occur.
  • Normally, the kidney has a positive NFP (approx +10 mmHg), driving filtration.


Other Options:
Option A: Incorrect calculation.
Option C: Positive NFP would require lower oncotic pressure.
Option D: Physiologically inconsistent with the values given.

stem: A physiology experiment records a graph of muscle contraction in response to increasing frequency of electrical stimulation. The graph shows individual twitches initially, then incomplete summation, followed by a smooth, sustained contraction at higher frequency. At which point on the graph does the maximum force of contraction occur?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Fused tetanus is the stage when rapid stimulation prevents relaxation.
  • This produces a smooth, sustained contraction with maximal force.
  • Mechanism: continuous Ca2+ availability keeps cross-bridge cycling active.
  • Skeletal muscle typically reaches maximum force in fused tetanus.


Other Options:
Option A: Single twitch generates minimal force.
Option B: Unfused tetanus allows partial relaxation, not maximum.
Option D: Fatigue decreases contractile force.

stem: A postoperative patient is receiving Intravenous fluids. After several liters of fluid administration, he develops hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis. Which of the following IV fluids is most likely responsible?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Infusion of large volumes of 0.9% saline leads to hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis.
  • High chloride content reduces plasma bicarbonate (dilutional effect).
  • This is a normal anion gap metabolic acidosis.
  • Balanced fluids like Ringer's lactate do not cause this issue.


Other Options:
Option A: DNS contains glucose, not the main cause.
Option B: Dextrose water = free water, no hyperchloremia.
Option D: Ringer's lactate buffers acidosis, not causes it.

stem: In a capillary fluid exchange experiment, the interstitial compartment is analyzed for its pressure dynamics. What is the typical value of Interstitial hydrostatic pressure in most tissues under normal physiological conditions?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Normal interstitial hydrostatic pressure = slightly negative (-2 mmHg).
  • Maintained by lymphatic drainage and tissue compliance.
  • Negative pressure prevents edema by pulling fluid into lymphatics.
  • This is one of the Starling forces regulating capillary exchange.


Other Options:
Option B: Positive pressure would promote edema.
Option C: Zero is theoretical, not physiological.
Option D: Slightly positive only in closed compartments.

stem: Which of the following contributes to high altitude cerebral edema?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
  • At altitude, low PO2 (hypoxia) occurs.
  • Hypoxia cerebral vasodilation → increased blood flow and capillary pressure.
  • This leads to vasogenic cerebral edema.
  • Symptoms: headache, confusion, ataxia.


Other Options:
Option B: Not relevant; high PO2 does not occur at altitude.
Option C: Incorrect; vessels respond to hypoxia, not hyperoxia.
Option D: Hypoxia causes vasodilation, not vasoconstriction.

stem: In a ventilation-perfusion (V/Q) ratio graph, four points labeled A, B, C, and D represent different regions of the lung. One of the points corresponds to a region with complete obstruction of pulmonary blood flow (pulmonary embolism). Which of the following points most accurately represents this condition?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Pulmonary embolism = no perfusion but ventilation intact.
  • V/Q ratio → ∞ (dead space ventilation).
  • On graphs, this corresponds to point D.
  • Clinical correlation: Increased dead space is diagnostic of embolism.


Other Options:
Option A, B & C: Represent base, apex, or shunt, not embolism.

stem: A man has climbed a mountain for trekking. What would be the acid-base variation that can be found due to physiological response in this individual?
subject: Physiology
explanation:
Solution
  • At altitude, hypoxia stimulates hyperventilation.
  • This decrease in CO2 → respiratory alkalosis.
  • Kidneys compensate by excreting bicarbonate (chronic adaptation).
  • Early symptoms include dizziness, tingling.


Other Options:
Option A: Not the primary disturbance.
Option B: Alkalosis may occur later as compensation, not initially.
Option D: Hypoventilation/retained CO2 not expected.

stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and visible atrophy of muscles on the right side of her body, including fasciculations. Neurological examination shows hypotonia, decreased reflexes, and muscle wasting, all confined to the right side. Sensory examination is normal. Which of the following is the most likely site of the lesion?
options: Right anterior horn of spinal cord
Right corticospinal tract in the medulla
subject: Neurology
explanation:
Solution
  • The anterior horn of the spinal cord houses lower motor neurons (LMN).
  • LMN lesion signs: Fasciculations, hypotonia, areflexia, atrophy.
  • Sensory function remains intact (pure motor lesion).
  • Seen in poliomyelitis and ALS (LMN component).


Other Options:
Option A: Internal capsule lesion = UMN signs (spasticity, hyperreflexia).
Option C: Dorsal root ganglion → sensory loss, not motor.
Option D: Corticospinal tract lesion = UMN signs, not LMN.

stem: A patient with episodic headache, palpitations, and hypertension is diagnosed with pheochromocytoma. Which agent is used preoperatively to control blood pressure?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Phenoxybenzamine is a long-acting, irreversible non-selective alpha-blocker.
  • It is the drug of choice for preoperative control of hypertension in pheochromocytoma.
  • It prevents catecholamine-induced hypertensive crisis during surgery.
  • alpha-blockade is always initiated before beta-blockade to avoid unopposed alpha-adrenergic vasoconstriction.


Other Options:
Option B: Esmolol (beta-blocker) can worsen hypertension if given before alpha-blockade.
Option C: Aspirin has no role in pheochromocytoma.
Option D: Clonidine reduces sympathetic outflow but is not effective for tumor-induced catecholamine surge.

stem: What is the mechanism of action of Pegloticase?
options: Inhibition of URAT1 in renal tubules
Oxidation of uric acid to allantoin
Inhibition of microtubule polymerization in neutrophils
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Pegloticase is a recombinant uricase enzyme.
  • It converts uric acid to allantoin, which is water-soluble and excreted easily.
  • Used in chronic, refractory gout unresponsive to standard therapy.
  • Reduces uric acid burden and tophus size.


Other Options:
Option A: Describes mechanism of allopurinol/febuxostat.
Option B: Describes mechanism of probenecid/lesinurad.
Option D: Describes the mechanism of colchicine.

stem: A 60-year-old male with chronic kidney disease presents with normocytic normochromic anemia. What is the most appropriate management?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • CKD anemia is caused by erythropoietin deficiency.
  • Darbepoetin alfa is a long-acting EPO analogue that stimulates RBC production.
  • It improves hemoglobin levels and reduces transfusion requirements.
  • Iron supplementation may be added only if deficiency exists.


Other Options:
Option B: Parenteral iron is for iron-deficiency anemia, not CKD anemia.
Option C: Oral iron is ineffective unless deficiency coexists.
Option D: Transfusions are used in severe/acute anemia, not long-term CKD.

stem: A young student presents with high-grade fever, headache, and nuchal rigidity. CSF shows gram-negative diplococci. What is the drug of choice for chemoprophylaxis of meningococcal meningitis?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Rifampicin is the drug of choice for prophylaxis of meningococcal meningitis.
  • It eliminates nasopharyngeal carriage of Neisseria meningitidis.
  • Alternative agents: Ciprofloxacin or Ceftriaxone (if rifampicin contraindicated).
  • Used in close contacts and outbreak settings.


Other Options:
Option A: Amoxicillin does not eradicate the carrier state.
Option C: Ethambutol is an anti-TB drug.
Option D: Doxycycline is not used for meningococcal prophylaxis.

stem: A patient on Ipratropium and Salbutamol therapy presents with worsening symptoms. What is the next best agent to add?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • In bronchial asthma/COPD not controlled by short-acting agents (Ipratropium + Salbutamol), the next step is to add an inhaled corticosteroid (ICS) with a long-acting beta-2 agonist (LABA) such as Salmeterol.
  • ICS reduces airway inflammation and improves long-term outcomes.
  • LABA provides sustained bronchodilation but must always be combined with ICS in asthma.
  • Montelukast is add-on therapy in mild persistent asthma, not first escalation.


Other Options:
Option A: LAMA (e.g., Tiotropium) is used in COPD, not first-line step-up in asthma.
Option C: Montelukast is less effective than ICS+LABA.
Option D: Increasing SABA dose leads to tolerance and adverse effects.

stem: A patient on medications develops oral lichenoid lesions and oral ulcers. Which medication is responsible?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Propranolol, a non-selective beta-blocker, is known to cause oral lichenoid reactions and oral ulcers.
  • Mechanism: immunologic reaction resembling lichen planus.
  • It is a rare but important adverse drug reaction to recognize.


Other Options:
Option A: Lithium causes hypothyroidism, tremor and diabetes insipidus, but not oral lichenoid lesions.
Option C: Ciprofloxacin → tendon rupture, cartilage damage.
Option D: Fluconazole hepatotoxicity, drug interactions via CYP inhibition.

stem: In the given image (phases of cardiac action potential), which of the following is the site of action of Amiodarone?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Amiodarone is a Class III antiarrhythmic drug.
  • It prolongs phase 3 (repolarization) of the cardiac action potential by blocking K+ channels.
  • This increases the action potential duration and effective refractory period, suppressing re-entry arrhythmias.


Other Options:
Option A: Phase 0 involves Na+ influx, blocked by Class I drugs.
Option B: Phase 2 involves Ca2+ influx, blocked by Class IV drugs.
Option D: Phase 4 involves pacemaker slope, modified by Class II drugs.

stem: A patient with a history of GERD was prescribed a prokinetic agent. Which of the agents will increase the motility and tone of the intestine?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Metoclopramide is a D2 receptor antagonist with prokinetic action.
  • It increases ACh release in the GI tract enhances gastric emptying and intestinal motility.
  • Used in GERD, gastroparesis, and antiemetic therapy.


Other Options:
Option B: Vonoprazan = potassium-competitive acid blocker (not prokinetic).
Option C: Omeprazole = PPI, reduces acid secretion, not motility.
Option D: Magaldrate = antacid, no prokinetic effect.

stem: A 40-year-old man with HIV and rifampicin-resistant TB. CD4 count is 110. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Start BPALM regimen for 6 months and follow up for IRIS
Start HRZS with streptomycin for 9 months
Start HRZE with linezolid for 9 months
Start kanamycin-based MDR regimen for 20 months
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • WHO 2022 guidelines recommend BPaLM regimen (Bedaquiline, Pretomanid, Linezolid, Moxifloxacin) for MDR-TB including rifampicin-resistant TB.
  • Duration = 6 months, highly effective and safer than older regimens.
  • HIV patients need close monitoring for IRIS (immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome).


Other Options:
Option B: Outdated regimen.
Option C: HRZE+linezolid is not standard.
Option D: Long kanamycin-based regimens are no longer preferred.

stem: A patient with T2DM presented with RBS 500 mg/dL, Na 126, K 4.3. Which of the following steps is NOT used in management?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Management of DKA/HHS: IV fluids (0.9% saline), insulin infusion, potassium monitoring.
  • Hypertonic 3% saline is never used in DKA/HHS.
  • Correct Na+ for hyperglycemia before deciding fluid.


Other Options:
Option A: Insulin is the cornerstone.
Option B: Monitoring K+ critical as insulin shifts K+ into cells.
Option C: 0.9% NS is the initial fluid of choice.

stem: A patient with fever, cough, weight loss, CXR shows RUL infiltrates and sputum is AFB+. He is HIV positive. Preferred management?
options: Start ART after 2 weeks of ATT
Start ART after 1 week of ATT
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • HIV with active TB: ATT is started first.
  • ART initiation after 2 weeks reduces mortality while lowering risk of IRIS.
  • Too early increases IRIS risk; too late leads to progression of HIV.


Other Options:
Option B: Both together increases IRIS risk.
Option C: 1 week is too early.
Option D: Delaying ART till after ATT completion worsens HIV prognosis.

stem: A patient on Chlorthalidone develops renal stone. What is the mechanism of stone formation?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Thiazides (chlorthalidone) increase calcium reabsorption but cause hypocitraturia.
  • Citrate normally binds calcium, preventing stone formation.
  • Citrate more free Ca2+ - stone risk.


Other Options:
Option B: Oxalate excretion not affected.
Option C: Thiazides actually urinary Ca2+ excretion (prevent stones otherwise).
Option D: Incorrect.

stem: A bronchial asthma patient with palpitations and chest discomfort was diagnosed with paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia. Preferred drug?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT) is commonly due to AV nodal re-entry.
  • The first-line treatment in a hemodynamically stable patient is IV Adenosine.
  • Adenosine causes transient AV nodal block, terminating re-entrant tachycardia.
  • It has an extremely short half-life (less than 10 seconds).
  • Although it may cause brief bronchospasm, it is still preferred over beta-blockers in asthmatics.
  • Therefore, IV Adenosine is the drug of choice.


Other Options:
Option A - Oral Diltiazem
  • Not useful for acute termination of PSVT.
  • Oral route is too slow in emergency settings.
  • IV form may be used if adenosine is contraindicated.

Option B Oral Adenosine
  • Adenosine is not given orally due to rapid metabolism.
  • Only effective when administered intravenously.
  • Hence Incorrect.

Option D-IV Propranolol
  • Non-selective beta-blocker.
  • Can precipitate bronchospasm in asthma patients.
  • Not preferred in bronchial asthma.

stem: In a rural village, multiple individuals developed dysentery after consuming raw, unpasteurized milk. Stool microscopy revealed Gram-negative, comma or S-shaped rods along with abundant polymorphonuclear leukocytes. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism for this outbreak?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Campylobacter jejuni is a curved, motile Gram-negative bacillus.
  • Transmission commonly occurs via unpasteurized milk and undercooked poultry.
  • Produces inflammatory dysentery with fever, cramps, and stools containing blood, mucus, and PMNs.
  • Pathogenesis involves mucosal invasion and cytotoxin production.
  • Important sequelae include Guillain-Barré syndrome and reactive arthritis.


Other Options:
Option A: Clostridium perfringens Toxin-mediated watery diarrhea; no fecal PMNs.
Option B: Staphylococcus aureus Preformed enterotoxin; rapid vomiting and diarrhea.
Option C: Vibrio parahaemolyticus From seafood; watery diarrhea, not invasive dysentery.

stem: A 30-year-old male agricultural worker from a rural area presents with a chronic swelling of the foot associated with multiple discharging sinuses. On examination, black granules were seen in the discharge. Microscopic examination of these granules will show which of the following morphologies?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Mycetoma (Madura foot) is a chronic granulomatous infection of skin and subcutaneous tissue.
  • Caused by fungi (Eumycetoma) or actinomycetes (Actinomycetoma).
  • Black granules in discharge are typical of Eumycetoma.
  • Microscopy shows pigmented, septate hyphae inside grains.
  • The disease progresses slowly, producing sinus tracts, deformity, and disability.


Other Options:
  • Arthrospores: Seen in dermatophytes, not mycetoma.
  • Pseudohyphae: Typical of Candida.
  • Flower-like septate hyphae: Classic for Sporothrix schenckii in culture.

stem: A 4-year-old child is brought to the clinic with complaints of intense perianal itching, especially at night. The mother also reports seeing thread-like worms in the child's inner garments. A perianal cellophane tape test is performed in the early morning, and the microscopic image shows the diagnostic morphology. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) is the commonest helminth infection in children.
  • Female worms migrate to the perianal region at night to lay eggs, causing nocturnal perianal pruritus.
  • Diagnosis: Cellophane tape test shows oval, flattened (planoconvex) eggs.
  • Transmission occurs by autoinfection and contamination of hands, bedding, and clothes.
  • The infection spreads rapidly in schools and households.


Other Options:
Option A: Hymenolepis nana
  • Dwarf tapeworm; causes diarrhea/abdominal pain, not perianal itching.

Option C: Trichuris trichiura
  • Whipworm; dysentery and rectal prolapse; eggs are barrel-shaped with bipolar plugs.

stem: A 19-year-old college student suddenly developed fever, headache, vomiting, and altered sensorium. He was diagnosed with meningitis and died within 24 hours. A postmortem examination of the brain showed purulent exudates over the convexities and base of the brain, especially around the cerebral vessels. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Neisseria meningitidis is a Gram-negative diplococcus and the most common cause of epidemic meningitis in adolescents and young adults.
  • Rapid onset of fever, headache, vomiting, and altered sensorium is typical; death can occur within hours to a day.
  • At autopsy, purulent exudates are characteristically seen over the convexities and base of the brain, with vessel involvement.
  • Transmission occurs via respiratory droplets; outbreaks are common in hostels, barracks, and colleges.
  • Complications include Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (adrenal hemorrhage → shock, DIC).


Other Options:
Option A: Poliovirus
  • Causes aseptic meningitis or paralysis, not purulent bacterial meningitis.

Option B: Mycobacterium tuberculosis
  • Produces basal exudates, but disease is chronic and indolent, not fulminant.

Option D: Cryptococcus neoformans
  • It causes subacute meningitis in immunocompromised patients, not rapid fatal illness.

stem: A 35-year-old man presents with low-grade fever, weight loss, and generalized lymphadenopathy. He works in an old building undergoing renovation. Chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. A lymph node biopsy shows small intracellular yeast-like organisms within macrophages on GMS stain. SDA growth shows the given morphology. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Histoplasma capsulatum is a dimorphic fungus found in soil enriched with bird or bat droppings.
  • Infection occurs via inhalation of microconidia → phagocytosed by macrophages appear as small intracellular yeasts.
  • Presents with bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, low-grade fever, and weight loss, mimicking tuberculosis.
  • Disseminated disease may involve liver, spleen, and bone marrow, especially in immunocompromised hosts.
  • Characteristic lab finding: growth on Sabouraud's agar and intracellular yeasts on GMS stain.


Other Options:
Option A: Cryptococcus neoformans
  • Encapsulated yeast, detected by India ink; associated with meningitis in AIDS.

Option C: Blastomyces dermatitidis
  • Broad-based budding yeast, pulmonary + skin lesions.

Option D: Pneumocystis jirovecii
  • Atypical fungus; causes pneumonia in HIV, not lymphadenopathy.

stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with painful vesicular lesions around her lips that progressed to shallow ulcers. She also complains of low-grade fever and malaise. A smear from the lesion base reveals multinucleated giant cells and intranuclear inclusions. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Herpes simplex virus (HSV-1) typically causes orolabial herpes (cold sores).
  • Characterized by painful vesicles that rupture to form shallow ulcers.
  • Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells with Cowdry type A intranuclear inclusions.
  • The virus establishes latency in the trigeminal ganglion, with periodic reactivation. Triggers include fever, stress, menstruation, or sunlight exposure.


Other Options:
Option A: HPV Causes warts; no vesicles or giant cells.
Option C: CMV Produces large cells with owl's eye inclusions, not multinucleated cells in vesicular lesions.
Option D: EBV Causes infectious mononucleosis; atypical lymphocytes, not vesicular ulcers.

stem: A farmer presents with a painless ulcer with a black eschar on his forearm, along with significant surrounding edema. A smear from the lesion is stained using McFadyean stain and shows large rectangular Gram-positive bacilli with a purplish capsule. Which of the following is the most likely causative organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Bacillus anthracis causes cutaneous anthrax, the most common form of anthrax.
  • Presents with a painless ulcer that progresses to a black necrotic eschar surrounded by massive edema.
  • The McFadyean Stain highlights the polypeptide capsule (D-glutamic acid) in purple.
  • Bacillus anthracis is a large, non-motile, spore-forming Gram-positive bacillus.
  • Virulence factors: capsule (anti-phagocytic) and anthrax toxin (PA, EF, LF).


Other Options:
Option A: Clostridium perfringens It causes gas gangrene with crepitus, not black eschar.
Option C: Yersinia pestis Bubonic plague with buboes, not localized eschar.
Option D: Francisella tularensis It causes ulceroglandular tularemia, but no purple capsule on stain.

stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with her third episode of meningitis in the past 2 years. Each episode was confirmed to be caused by Neisseria meningitidis. There is no significant past medical history otherwise. Her vaccination history is up to date, and she is HIV negative. Which of the following investigations is most appropriate to evaluate the underlying cause of her recurrent infections?
subject: Immunology
explanation:
Solution
  • Recurrent Neisseria meningitidis infections suggest a defect in the complement pathway.
  • Deficiency of terminal complement components (C5-C9) predisposes to repeated meningococcal infections.
  • The most appropriate test: CH50 assay, which measures total complement activity.
  • Normal CH50 rules out complement deficiency and points to other immune defects.
  • Patients often otherwise appear healthy and have normal immunoglobulin levels.


Other Options:
Option A: Serum immunoglobulin levels
  • Detects antibody deficiencies; not linked to recurrent meningococcal infections.

Option B: HIV ELISA
  • Excludes HIV but not relevant here (patient already HIV negative).

Option D: Neutrophil oxidative burst test
  • Used for chronic granulomatous disease; recurrent bacterial/fungal infections, not meningococci specifically.

stem: Which of the following factors helps Group A Streptococcus adhere to the nasopharyngeal epithelium?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Lipoteichoic acid on the surface of Streptococcus pyogenes (Group A strep) mediates adherence to epithelial cells.
  • It binds to fibronectin receptors on host cell membranes.
  • Adherence is the first step in pathogenesis, allowing colonization of the nasopharynx.
  • Works synergistically with M protein (anti-phagocytic) for virulence.
  • Responsible for initiation of diseases like pharyngitis, impetigo, and invasive infections.


Other Options:
Option A: Capsule hyaluronic acid capsule prevents phagocytosis, not adherence.
Option B: Flagella streptococci are non-motile.
Option C: Lipoprotein not involved in adhesion for this organism.

stem: A person from an endemic state presents with a history of lymphedema. Smear shows the given Image. What is the mechanism of lymphedema?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Lymphedema in endemic regions is most commonly due to filariasis (Wuchereria bancrofti).
  • Adult worms lodge in lymphatic channels, causing mechanical obstruction.
  • This results in accumulation of lymph, fibrosis, and the development of elephantiasis.
  • The edema is non-pitting and progressive if untreated.
  • Classic exam clue: Microfilariae show sheathed forms with nuclei not reaching the tail tip.


Other Options:
Option A: Hypoalbuminemia causes generalized pitting edema, not localized lymphedema.
Option B: Hypoproteinemia similar to hypoalbuminemia; systemic edema, not lymphatic blockage.
Option D: Increased hydrostatic pressure seen in CHF or venous stasis, not filarial lymphedema.

stem: A 25-year-old sewage worker with a history of fever for 1 week and weakness since 1 day. Lab Investigations show elevated bilirubin. The patient has decreased urine output and conjunctival hemorrhages. What is your most likely diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Weil's disease is the severe icteric form of leptospirosis caused by Leptospira interrogans.
  • Characterized by jaundice, renal failure, hemorrhagic manifestations, and hypotension.
  • High-risk groups: sewage workers, farmers, people exposed to contaminated water.
  • Key feature: conjunctival suffusion (reddening without exudate), an important diagnostic clue.
  • Severe disease may progress to multi-organ failure if untreated.


Other Options:
Option B: Brucellosis Causes undulant fever, hepatosplenomegaly; no jaundice with hemorrhage.
Option C: DHF Produces thrombocytopenia and plasma leakage; not associated with renal failure and jaundice.
Option D: Enteric fever Step-ladder fever, rose spots, splenomegaly, lacks jaundice and hemorrhage.

stem: A patient presents with an abscess. Pus sample collected shows Gram-positive thin filamentous branching structures, which are acid-fast on modified ZN stain and paraffin bait technique was used for diagnosis. Which is the most likely implicated organism?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Nocardia species are aerobic, branching, filamentous, Gram-positive bacteria.
  • They are weakly acid-fast on modified Ziehl-Neelsen stain.
  • Classic diagnostic method: paraffin bait technique, as they can metabolize paraffin.
  • Causes pulmonary nocardiosis, brain abscesses, and cutaneous infections, often in immunocompromised hosts.
  • Differentiated from Actinomyces by being aerobic and partially acid-fast.


Other Options:
Option A: Mycobacteria acid-fast bacilli, but not filamentous branching organisms.
Option B: Aspergillus septate hyphae; fungus, not acid-fast.
Option D: Actinomyces branching filaments, but anaerobic and non-acid-fast.

stem: Caspases are crucial enzymes involved in which of the following forms of programmed cell death?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • Caspases are proteolytic enzymes that play a central role in programmed cell death.
  • In apoptosis, initiator caspases such as 8 and 9 activate executioner caspases like 3, 6, and 7, which dismantle the cell in an orderly way.
  • In pyroptosis, caspase-1 (and related inflammatory caspases) mediates cell death by activating inflammatory pathways and forming pores in the membrane.
  • In contrast, necroptosis is a caspase-independent pathway mediated by RIPK1 and RIPK3 kinases.
  • Thus, caspases are specific to apoptosis and pyroptosis.


Other Options:
Option B: Necroptosis is caspase-independent, so caspases are not crucial here.
Option C: Pyroptosis involves caspases, but necroptosis does not.
Option D: Again incorrect because necroptosis does not require caspases.

stem: A 20-year-old woman presents with mild anemia and jaundice. Her father has a history of a similar blood disorder. Laboratory findings show increased mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • Hereditary spherocytosis is an inherited defect in red blood cell membrane proteins such as ankyrin and spectrin.
  • This defect leads to the formation of spherical red cells with reduced deformability and increased fragility.
  • A key laboratory feature is an increased MCHC, reflecting the compact nature of the spherocytes.
  • Patients typically present with hemolytic anemia, jaundice, and splenomegaly, often with a positive family history.
  • Splenectomy is curative in severe cases.


Other Options:
Option A: This condition causes microcytic hypochromic anemia, not increased MCHC.
Option B: This disorder is acquired and usually Coombs test positive, unlike in hereditary spherocytosis.
Option D: This anemia is characterized by low MCHC, not high MCHC.

stem: A 32-year-old female presents with easy bruising, gum bleeding, and persistent nosebleeds. Peripheral smear is shown. What is the translocation?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • The clinical presentation and smear findings suggest acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL).
  • This leukemia is characterized by the translocation t(15;17), which results in the PML-RARA fusion gene.
  • The fusion blocks differentiation of promyelocytes, leading to an accumulation of abnormal cells.
  • APL is associated with a high risk of disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC) due to procoagulant granules in promyelocytes.
  • The mainstay of treatment is all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA), which induces differentiation of leukemic cells, often combined with arsenic trioxide.


Other Options:
Option A: This translocation is seen in AML with maturation (M2), not APL.
Option B: This abnormality defines chronic myeloid leukemia and is also seen in some acute lymphoblastic leukemias.
Option D: This translocation is characteristic of mantle cell lymphoma.

stem: A 46-year-old woman presents with a solitary thyroid mass. Histology shows immunostaining with chromogranin A and synaptophysin. Congo red staining reveals amyloid. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • Medullary thyroid carcinoma arises from parafollicular C cells of the thyroid.
  • It characteristically produces calcitonin, which deposits as amyloid in the stroma, detected with Congo red staining.
  • Immunohistochemistry shows positivity for neuroendocrine markers such as chromogranin A and synaptophysin.
  • Medullary carcinoma may occur sporadically or in association with MEN2A or MEN2B syndromes due to RET mutations.
  • Surgical removal of the thyroid is essential, and genetic screening of family members is recommended.


Other Options:
Option A: This carcinoma shows papillary structures, Orphan Annie nuclei, and psammoma bodies, not amyloid.
Option C: This tumor demonstrates capsular or vascular invasion but does not produce amyloid.
Option D: This carcinoma is undifferentiated, highly aggressive, and lacks neuroendocrine markers.

stem: A 32-year-old male has painless swelling in the right testis. Histopathology shows classic features. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • Seminoma is the most common germ cell tumor of the testis, typically occurring in young men.
  • Grossly, the tumor is well-circumscribed and homogenous without hemorrhage or necrosis.
  • Microscopically, it consists of large uniform cells with clear cytoplasm and central nuclei, often described as a "fried egg" appearance.
  • Seminomas are radiosensitive and have an excellent prognosis compared to non-seminomatous germ cell tumors.
  • They often show positivity for placental alkaline phosphatase (PLAP).


Other Options:
Option A: This carcinoma is more aggressive, frequently hemorrhagic and necrotic, and lacks the uniform cell appearance of seminoma.
Option C: This tumor produces alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) and demonstrates Schiller-Duval bodies.
Option D: This tumor produces beta-hCG and is highly aggressive, often with widespread metastasis and hemorrhage.

stem: Which of the following diseases will follow the given pedigree chart?
subject: Genetics
explanation:
Solution
  • Kearns-Sayre syndrome is a mitochondrial disorder.
  • Mitochondria are inherited exclusively through the maternal line, so mitochondrial diseases show maternal inheritance patterns on pedigree charts.
  • Clinical features include progressive external ophthalmoplegia, pigmentary retinopathy, cardiac conduction abnormalities, and muscle weakness.
  • The hallmark in pedigrees is that both males and females can be affected, but transmission occurs only through affected mothers, never through affected fathers.


Other Options:
Option A: This syndrome is due to paternal chromosome 15 deletion with maternal imprinting, not mitochondrial inheritance.
Option B: This syndrome is caused by a microdeletion on chromosome 7, and follows autosomal dominant inheritance.
Option C: This condition is an autosomal dominant skeletal dysplasia, not maternally inherited.

stem: A 68-year-old man presents with generalized lymphadenopathy and leukocytosis. What is the investigation of choice to confirm chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL)?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • The diagnosis of chronic lymphocytic leukemia is confirmed by flow cytometry of peripheral blood.
  • This test demonstrates a clonal population of B lymphocytes with characteristic immunophenotype, including CD5, CD19, CD20, and CD23 positivity.
  • Bone marrow biopsy is not required for diagnosis but may be done for staging.
  • CLL commonly presents in elderly men with lymphocytosis, lymphadenopathy, and sometimes hepatosplenomegaly.


Other Options:
Option A: This biopsy can support staging but is not the first-line diagnostic test.
Option C: Serum LDH may be elevated but is nonspecific and not diagnostic.
Option D: Lymph node biopsy is more relevant in lymphomas, not necessary for CLL.

stem: A 65-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with bleeding per vagina. She undergoes hysterectomy. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Postmenopausal bleeding is considered endometrial carcinoma until proven otherwise.
  • This malignancy arises from the endometrial lining of the uterus, most commonly associated with prolonged estrogen exposure, obesity, diabetes, and nulliparity.
  • Patients usually present with abnormal uterine bleeding, and histology confirms the diagnosis after hysterectomy.
  • It is the most common gynecologic malignancy in developed countries.


Other Options:
Option A: This condition is rare in postmenopausal women and typically causes pelvic pain, not postmenopausal bleeding.
Option B: Fibroids generally regress after menopause due to lack of estrogen.
Option C: This condition is most common in multiparous women of reproductive age and does not typically present after menopause.

stem: A 35-year-old woman undergoes imaging for right upper quadrant pain. A liver mass is excised. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • Focal nodular hyperplasia (FNH) is a benign lesion of the liver, usually discovered incidentally or when investigating abdominal pain.
  • Grossly, it is characterized by a well-circumscribed nodule with a central stellate scar.
  • Histologically, it consists of benign hepatocytes arranged around a fibrous central scar containing abnormal blood vessels.
  • Unlike hepatic adenoma, FNH has no malignant potential and does not rupture or bleed.


Other Options:
Option A: This malignancy shows trabecular growth pattern, vascular invasion, and risk factors such as hepatitis B, hepatitis C, or cirrhosis.
Option C: This benign tumor is associated with oral contraceptive use and carries a risk of rupture and hemorrhage.
Option D: Secondary tumors in the liver are multiple and irregular, not solitary with a central scar.

stem: Which of the following conditions is least associated with hemosiderin-laden macrophages (heart failure cells)?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • Hemosiderin-laden macrophages, also called heart failure cells, are found in the lungs when red blood cells leak into alveoli due to increased pulmonary venous pressure.
  • These macrophages ingest hemoglobin and convert it into hemosiderin.
  • They are characteristically seen in conditions causing pulmonary congestion such as left-sided heart failure and pulmonary venous obstruction.
  • Protein-losing enteropathy does not lead to pulmonary congestion, so it is not associated with these cells.


Other Options:
Option A: Left-sided heart failure leads to pulmonary venous congestion and red cell leakage, producing heart failure cells.
Option C: Pulmonary venous embolism also increases venous pressure and can cause alveolar hemorrhage with hemosiderin-laden macrophages.
Option D: Right heart failure indirectly contributes through venous congestion, although less prominent than left-sided failure.

stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with Raynaud phenomenon, tight skin, and breathlessness. Anti-Scl-70 is positive. What feature is strongly associated?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Anti-Scl-70 antibodies (anti-topoisomerase 1) are strongly associated with diffuse systemic sclerosis.
  • The most significant systemic complication is interstitial lung disease, leading to pulmonary fibrosis.
  • Patients present with breathlessness and restrictive lung disease on pulmonary function tests.
  • Pulmonary hypertension can also occur and contributes to morbidity and mortality.


Other Options:
Option B: This is a feature of limited systemic sclerosis (CREST syndrome), not diffuse systemic sclerosis.
Option C: Inflammatory myositis may occur in overlap syndromes but is not strongly linked with anti-Scl-70.
Option D: Sicca symptoms are more characteristic of Sjögren syndrome.

stem: A 48-year-old woman has dry eyes, dry mouth, and difficulty swallowing. Anti-Ro/SSA antibodies are positive. What is the characteristic feature of this disease?
options: Lymphocytic infiltration of salivary glands with acinar destruction
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • The presentation and antibody profile are consistent with Sjögren syndrome.
  • The hallmark lesion is lymphocytic infiltration of salivary and lacrimal glands, leading to acinar destruction and glandular dysfunction.
  • This causes keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eye) and xerostomia (dry mouth).
  • Patients often have difficulty swallowing dry foods and may develop parotid gland enlargement.
  • There is also an increased risk of developing non-Hodgkin lymphoma.


Other Options:
Option A: Fibrosis occurs late but is not the primary lesion.
Option C: Neutrophilic abscess is typical of bacterial sialadenitis.
Option D: Amyloid deposition is seen in systemic amyloidosis, not Sjögren syndrome.

stem: A 45-year-old man presents with leg weakness and seizures. Histology shows Antoni A and B areas. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • Schwannoma is a benign nerve sheath tumor composed of Schwann cells.
  • Histologically, it shows a mixture of Antoni A areas (cellular, palisading nuclei, Verocay bodies) and Antoni B areas (loose, hypocellular regions).
  • Schwannomas commonly arise on cranial nerves, especially the vestibulocochlear nerve (acoustic neuroma).
  • They may present with hearing loss, tinnitus, or balance problems when involving cranial nerve VIII (Vestibulocochlear nerve).


Other Options:
Option A: This tumor arises from meningothelial cells and shows psammoma bodies, not Antoni areas.
Option C: This aggressive glioma shows pseudopalisading necrosis and high mitotic activity.
Option D: This tumor shows round nuclei with perinuclear halos ("fried-egg" appearance) and a delicate capillary network.

stem: A 50-year-old woman with autoimmune thyroid disease presents with jaundice and xanthelasma. AMA is positive. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Primary biliary cholangitis (previously called cirrhosis) is an autoimmune disease that primarily affects intrahepatic bile ducts.
  • The disease is strongly associated with antimitochondrial antibodies (AMA).
  • Clinical features include fatigue, pruritus, jaundice, and complications of chronic cholestasis such as xanthelasma and fat-soluble vitamin deficiency.
  • It is more common in middle-aged women and is associated with other autoimmune conditions like autoimmune thyroid disease.


Other Options:
Option A: This disease affects extrahepatic and intrahepatic bile ducts, and is associated with p-ANCA, not AMA.
Option C: This condition is associated with ANA and smooth muscle antibodies, not AMA.
Option D: This condition is due to medications causing cholestasis but does not involve AMA positivity.

stem: A 58-year-old patient has prolonged PT and Factor IX deficiency. In which condition will parenteral vitamin K improve this?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Vitamin K is required for the gamma-carboxylation of clotting factors II, VII, IX, and X.
  • In bile duct obstruction, fat absorption is impaired, leading to vitamin K deficiency.
  • Administering parenteral vitamin K corrects the deficiency and normalizes clotting factor synthesis, thereby improving prolonged PT.
  • Hepatitis A may cause hepatic dysfunction but does not specifically improve with vitamin K.
  • Hemophilia B is a genetic deficiency of factor IX and will not respond to vitamin K supplementation.


Other Options:
Option A: Liver disease reduces factor production and cannot be corrected simply with vitamin K.
Option B: This disorder causes vitamin B12 deficiency, unrelated to vitamin K metabolism.
Option D: This is a congenital factor IX deficiency and will not improve with vitamin K administration.

stem: A 58-year-old diabetic male is admitted to the ICU with ARDS following COVID-19 infection. He died 7 days after admission. As part of surveillance, the clinician records the death in the hospital records and this data is reported to health authorities. Which type of surveillance does this scenario represent?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • In passive surveillance, routine reporting of health data by healthcare facilities is sent to health authorities without additional active data collection efforts.
  • Here, the clinician simply recorded and forwarded the case, which matches passive reporting.
  • It is the most common and cost-effective method of surveillance used in public health.
  • However, passive surveillance may have under-reporting and lower sensitivity compared to active methods.
  • In contrast, active surveillance requires dedicated staff to actively search for cases.
  • Clinical correlation: Passive surveillance forms the backbone of national disease monitoring systems like IDSP (Integrated Disease Surveillance Program) in India.


Other Options:
Option A: It involves actively searching for cases by health authorities; not applicable here.
Option C: It involves selected institutions for monitoring specific diseases; not the scenario described.
Option D: It tracks syndromes/symptoms rather than confirmed diagnoses; not the method here.

stem: An urban area has a total population of 7,000,000 (70 lakhs), out of which 30% live in slum areas. As per the National Urban Health Mission (NUHM) norms, how many Urban Primary Health Centres (UPHCs) should be established?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • NUHM recommends 1 UPHC per 50,000 population in urban areas, with emphasis on underserved and slum populations.
  • Slum population here = 30% of 70,00,000 = 21,00,000.
  • Required UPHCs = 21,00,000 / 50,000 = 42. Thus, the correct answer is 42.
  • Clinical correlation: Urban slums are prone to infectious diseases, malnutrition and poor sanitation, making UPHCs essential for primary care.
  • High-yield: NUHM = UPHC: 50k population, UCHC: 2.5 lakh population.


Other Options:
Option A: Too low; underestimates actual requirement.
Option B: Incorrect calculation of catchment population.
Option D: Overestimates required UPHCs.

stem: In a research study, the mean hemoglobin levels of two independent populations are compared to assess the effect of two different dietary interventions. Which of the following statistical tests is most appropriate to compare the means between these two independent groups?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • The unpaired t-test (independent t-test) is the correct statistical test to compare means between two independent groups when the outcome is continuous (e.g. hemoglobin levels) and normally distributed.
  • It checks if the difference between group means is statistically significant.
  • This test assumes random sampling and approximately equal variances across groups.
  • If variances are unequal, a modified version (Welch's test) can be used.
  • Clinical correlation: Commonly applied in research comparing two treatment groups (e.g. drug vs placebo, diet A vs diet B).


Other Options:
Option A: It is used for categorical variables, not continuous ones like hemoglobin.
Option B: Compares means of the same group at two different times, not independent groups.
Option D: It is used for comparing means of more than two groups, not just two.

stem: In the Nalgonda technique used for defluoridation of water, which of the following components is primarily used?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • The Nalgonda technique is the standard method used in India for defluoridation of water.
  • It uses a combination of alum (aluminium sulfate), lime and bleaching powder (as disinfectant).
  • Alum precipitates fluoride as insoluble complexes, while lime aids flocculation and maintains pH.
  • This method is cost-effective, simple and suitable for rural areas with high fluoride content.
  • Clinical correlation: Effective in reducing fluorosis prevalence in endemic districts of India.


Other Options:
Option A: It is used for disinfection, not for defluoridation.
Option B: No role in fluoride removal.
Option D: It is used for disinfection, not fluoride removal.

stem: A 12-year-old child from a rural area presents with mottling of teeth and joint stiffness. On testing, the fluoride content in the local drinking water is found to be 5 ppm (parts per million). Which of the following dietary measures can help in reducing fluoride absorption and mitigating fluorosis?
options: Low calcium, low vitamin C diet
High calcium, vitamin C and antioxidant-rich diet
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • Fluorosis occurs with more than 1.5 ppm fluoride in water.
  • High calcium intake reduces fluoride absorption by forming insoluble complexes in the gut.
  • Vitamin C promotes bone and collagen health, mitigating skeletal changes of fluorosis.
  • Antioxidants help reduce oxidative stress caused by fluoride toxicity.
  • Together, these reduce both dental and skeletal fluorosis severity.
  • Dietary modification is an adjunct to defluoridation and safe water supply.


Other Options:
Option A: It worsens fluorosis by reducing protective nutrients.
Option B: No protective effect on fluoride toxicity.
Option D: It is not directly linked to fluoride absorption reduction.

stem: A middle-aged man from Andhra Pradesh presents with bowing of legs, joint pain and stiffness. He is suspected to have skeletal fluorosis. Which of the following measures is not helpful in the management or prevention of this condition?
options: Using water from a flowing source (like river or spring)
Use of alum for water treatment
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • Skeletal fluorosis occurs due to chronic ingestion of water with > 3 ppm fluoride.
  • Preventive measures include safe water sources, awareness programs and early diagnosis.
  • Alum is used in the Nalgonda technique, but its role is for fluoride removal from water, not direct management of skeletal fluorosis.
  • Thus, the listed option is not effective for treatment.
  • Switching to a safe water supply is the most effective strategy.


Other Options:
Option A: It is an effective preventive measure.
Option B: It contains less fluoride; preventive.
Option C: It helps reduce morbidity.

stem: A 4-year-old girl presents with classical clinical features of rickets and has a history of no sun exposure. Which of the following national programs is primarily responsible for addressing and managing such nutritional deficiencies in children under 6 years of age?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • ICDS (Integrated Child Development Services) focuses on children under 6 years, providing supplementary nutrition, health check-ups, immunization, referral services and preschool education.
  • It directly addresses nutritional deficiencies like rickets, anemia and protein-energy malnutrition.
  • It is a flagship program for maternal and child health in India.
  • Vitamin D deficiency rickets can be mitigated by early nutritional interventions through ICDS.


Other Options:
Option A: It is focused on anemia control across age groups.
Option B: It is not a primary national program for children under 6 yrs.
Option D: It targets school-going children, not under-6 groups.

stem: A mosquito larva is observed floating parallel to the water surface and is noted to have lateral abdominal hairs. This larval posture and morphology are characteristic of which mosquito species?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • Anopheles larvae rest parallel to the water surface and have lateral palmate hairs that help them float.
  • They lack a respiratory siphon, unlike Aedes and Culex larvae.
  • This posture is diagnostic in entomology for species identification.
  • Anopheles mosquitoes are vectors for malaria, making this recognition high-yield.
  • Clinical correlation: Identifying larval morphology is crucial for vector control programs.


Other Options:
Option A: Larvae rest at an angle to water with a long siphon.
Option B: They also rest at an angle with a short siphon.
Option D: They attach to aquatic plants for respiration, not free-floating.

stem: According to the Indian dietary guidelines, what is the recommended daily cholesterol intake relative to energy consumption?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • As per Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) guidelines, daily cholesterol intake should not exceed 100 mg per 1000 kcal of diet.
  • This helps in prevention of atherosclerosis, ischemic heart disease and stroke.
  • The recommendation is based on correlation between dietary fat intake and cardiovascular risk.
  • Clinical correlation: Population-based cholesterol reduction is part of NCD prevention strategies in India.


Other Options:
Option A: Cholesterol is measured in mg, not kcal.
Option B: Wrong measurement unit.
Option C: Unit error.

stem: A health worker checks a vial of oral polio vaccine (OPV) before administration and observes that the inner square of the Vaccine Vial Monitor (VVM) has darkened but is still lighter than the outer circle. What should be the correct action?
options: Shake well and use the vaccine
Use the vaccine as it is still potent
Re-freeze the vaccine and reuse later
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • Vaccine Vial Monitors (VVMs) indicate cumulative heat exposure of vaccines.
  • If the inner square is lighter than the outer circle, the vaccine is potent and safe to use.
  • Once the inner square becomes darker than or equal to the outer circle, the vial should be discarded.
  • VVMs are vital for ensuring potency of OPV in field conditions.


Other Options:
Option A: Not required, vaccine is still usable.
Option B: OPV is a liquid, no need for shaking.
Option D: Re-freezing damages potency, never recommended.

stem: Which of the following vaccines is used as a preventive cancer vaccine under the national immunization program in India?
options: Hepatitis C vaccine - to prevent liver cancer
HPV vaccine - to prevent cervical cancer
BCG vaccine to prevent skin cancer
Pneumococcal vaccine - to prevent lung cancer
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • Human Papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine prevents infection with oncogenic HPV types, reducing risk of cervical cancer.
  • Introduced in the National Immunization Program in India in 2023-24.
  • It is the first cancer-preventive vaccine included in India's schedule.
  • Large-scale vaccination of adolescent girls is expected to reduce cervical cancer burden significantly.


Other Options:
Option A: No vaccine available yet.
Option C: Protects against tuberculosis, not skin cancer.
Option D: Prevents pneumococcal infections, not cancer.

stem: As per the Anemia Mukt Bharat guidelines, what is the recommended weekly dose of iron and folic acid for an 11-year-old child?
options: 100 mg elemental iron + 500 mcg folic acid
60 mg elemental iron + 500 mcg folic acid
30 mg elemental iron + 250 mcg folic acid
20 mg elemental iron + 100 mcg folic acid
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • For children 10-19 years, Anemia Mukt Bharat recommends 60 mg elemental iron + 500 mcg folic acid once weekly.
  • This helps in reducing the prevalence of iron deficiency anemia among adolescents.
  • Weekly supplementation ensures compliance and minimizes side effects compared to daily dosing.
  • School-based iron-folic acid supplementation is a key strategy in India's anemia control.


Other Options:
Option A: Excess dose for adolescents.
Option C: Recommended for younger children (5-9 years).
Option D: Too low, not effective for 11 years.

stem: Which of the following is the correct sequence of steps in conducting a Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)?
options: Selection of subjects -> Randomization -> Allocation -> Follow-up -> Analysis
Randomization -> Selection of subjects -> Allocation -> Follow-up -> Analysis
Selection of subjects -> Allocation -> Randomization -> Analysis -> Follow-up
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • The proper sequence of an RCT is: Selection of subjects → Randomization → Allocation into groups → Follow-up → Analysis.
  • Selection ensures the study population is representative. Randomization prevents bias and balances confounding variables.
  • Allocation places participants into intervention and control groups.
  • Follow-up monitors outcomes, and finally analysis interprets results.
  • RCTs are considered the gold standard in evaluating new drugs and interventions.


Other Options:
Option A: Randomization cannot occur after allocation.
Option C: Randomization must follow subject selection, not precede it.
Option D: Randomization must occur before allocation and follow-up.

stem: Which of the following is used to measure the extent of objective and target achievement and to assess the quality and impact of results obtained in a health program?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • Evaluation measures whether a program has achieved its objectives and targets and assesses the quality, efficiency and impact of the results.
  • It is an essential component of program management and policy-making. Evaluation can be process-based, impact-based or outcome-based.
  • For example, measuring reduction in maternal mortality after implementation of maternal health programs.


Other Options:
Option A: Initial step of program design, not assessment.
Option B: Continuous collection of health data, not target assessment.
Option C: Tracks progress during implementation, not final results.

stem: In a district-level survey, the introduction of breast cancer screening showed an increased 5-year survival rate, but autopsy data revealed no actual change in mortality. What type of bias does this represent?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • Lead time bias occurs when earlier detection of a disease (e.g. through screening) increases survival time from diagnosis without changing the actual time of death.
  • It gives a false impression that survival has improved, even though mortality remains unchanged.
  • It is a common problem in evaluating cancer screening programs.
  • Seen in mammography for breast cancer, PSA testing for prostate cancer, etc.


Other Options:
Option A: Bias due to including only survivors, not relevant here.
Option C: It occurs in hospital-based studies, unrelated.
Option D: It is related to unequal diagnostic scrutiny, not survival time.

stem: What is the time interval between acquiring an infection and reaching the period of maximum infectivity known as?
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • Generation time is the interval between acquisition of infection and the moment when the host reaches maximum infectivity.
  • It is closely related to the incubation period but focuses on infectivity, not symptoms.
  • This concept is essential in understanding epidemic spread and calculating R0 (basic reproductive number).
  • In diseases like influenza and measles, individuals may be most infectious before symptoms develop.


Other Options:
Option B: Duration during which an infected host can transmit disease, not peak infectivity.
Option C: Time between exposure and symptom onset, not infectivity.
Option D: Interval between symptom onset in primary and secondary cases.

stem: Under the Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, a "child" is defined as any person?
subject: Forensic Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • As per the POCSO Act, 2012, a child is defined as any person below 18 years of age, irrespective of gender.
  • The Act is gender-neutral and covers both boys and girls under 18 years.
  • This law was enacted to provide protection of children against sexual offences and to establish child-friendly procedures.
  • Any sexual activity with minors (<18 years) is considered a statutory offence even if consensual.


Other Options:
Option B: 16 years is not the cutoff under POCSO.
Option C: No distinction of 21 years vs 18 years.
Option D: Gender distinction not applicable.

stem: The talus develops from a single primary ossification center in which month of intrauterine life?
subject: Forensic Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • The talus ossifies from a single primary ossification center that appears around the 7th month of intrauterine life.
  • It is one of the tarsal bones important in forensic age estimation.
  • Bone ossification helps with gestational age determination during autopsies.


Other Options:
Option A: Too early for talus ossification.
Option B: Already ossified by this stage.
Option D: Late; ossification occurs earlier.

stem: In a case of asphyxial death, bruises were found at the neck above the thyroid cartilage and back and scapular region; scratch marks were found over the neck and face. What is the most probable type of asphyxial death?
subject: Forensic Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Throttling refers to manual strangulation using hands, forearm or elbow.
  • Typical signs include bruises above thyroid cartilage, nail/scratch marks and injuries over neck and face due to the victim's struggle.
  • Often associated with homicidal assault.
  • Throttling is commonly encountered in sexual crimes and homicidal violence.


Other Options:
Option A: Strangulation by bending the neck forcibly with knee/forearm.
Option B: Strangulation using a cord/rope tightened by a stick (tourniquet).
Option D: Constriction using a ligature around the neck, not bare hands.

stem: A patient came with a history of unknown poisoning presenting with rice watery stools, garlicky odor and vomiting. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Forensic Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Acute arsenic poisoning presents with rice-water stools, vomiting, severe dehydration and a characteristic garlicky odor.
  • It mimics cholera, hence called "pseudo-cholera".
  • Arsenic damages the gastrointestinal mucosa and causes shock.


Other Options:
Option B: It causes constipation and blue gum line, not diarrhea.
Option C: It causes luminous vomiting, garlic odor but no rice-water stools.
Option D: It causes severe purgation but not garlic odor.

stem: A 16-year-old girl was brought for medicolegal examination by her parents with alleged consensual intercourse by a 25-year-old male. The girl agrees to it. On examination, signs of recent sexual intercourse were found, with no signs of force or injury. Which of the following is true?
options: It is not rape as consent is given
Inform police as it is statutory rape and trial should be started
The patient cannot file the case
