subject: Forensic Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Sexual intercourse with a girl <18 years is considered statutory rape under POCSO Act & IPC Section 375, irrespective of consent.
  • Doctors are legally bound to inform police in such cases. Consent by a minor is not valid in law.
  • Such cases must be documented and reported as medicolegal cases.


Other Options:
Option A: Minor consent does not exempt rape.
Option C: Consent not valid below 18 years.
Option D: Patients can report, but law mandates police intimation.

stem: There was a leak of toxic fumes from a factory. A siren sound was heard with instructions to civilians. What would be the most appropriate next step?
options: Don't follow instructions & open the doors and windows
Close the doors and windows and seal cracks
Go out of house and inform others
subject: PSM
explanation:
Solution
  • In case of toxic gas leaks, the recommended step is to shelter indoors, close windows/doors and seal cracks to minimize inhalation.
  • This is part of disaster preparedness protocols.
  • Evacuation is considered only when instructed by authorities.
  • Such measures reduce exposure to cyanide, chlorine or ammonia gas leaks.


Other Options:
Option A: Increases exposure to toxic fumes.
Option C: Unsafe to go outdoors without clearance.
Option D: Waiting without sealing increases risk.

stem: Which of the following is true about the Zsako phenomenon?
options: Increase in potassium level in vitreous
Fragmentation of blood in retinal vessels
Collection of blood in dependent areas
subject: Forensic Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • The Zsako phenomenon refers to muscular/tendon contractions that persist for a short time after death when mechanical stimulation is applied.
  • It is a postmortem muscular response and helps confirm recent death.
  • This phenomenon is due to residual irritability of muscles and nerves.
  • Useful in forensic determination of time since death in a very early postmortem.


Other Options:
Option B: Vitreous potassium rise is used for estimating time since death, but not Zsako.
Option C: It is seen in air embolism, not Zsako.
Option D: It refers to hypostasis/livor mortis.

stem: Which components of binocular vision do these pictures represent?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
  • Binocular vision develops in stages:
  • 1. Simultaneous macular perception (SMP) → Both eyes perceive images at the same time.
  • 2. Fusion → The cortical integration of two slightly different retinal images into a single perception.
  • 3. Stereopsis → The highest grade; depth perception due to retinal disparity.
  • The given pictures illustrate both SMP and Fusion, but not stereopsis.


Other Options:
Option A: Only SMP: First step only, without fusion; incomplete here.
Option B: Only Fusion: Requires SMP first; cannot exist alone.
Option D: Stereopsis and Fusion: Depth perception isn't depicted here.

stem: An elderly patient presents with whitish dandruff-like scales at the pupillary margin and a ring on anterior lens capsule. Diagnosis:
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
  • Pseudoexfoliation syndrome (PEX) is an age-related systemic microfibrillopathy.
  • Eye findings: whitish dandruff-like deposits on the pupillary margin and a target/ring-shaped deposit on anterior lens capsule.
  • Important because it predisposes to secondary open-angle glaucoma and complications during cataract surgery (zonular weakness).


Other Options:
Option A: Fuch's Endothelial Dystrophy Corneal endothelial disease; guttate changes, corneal edema, not dandruff-like material.
Option B: Arcus Senilis Peripheral corneal opacity; unrelated to pupillary margin.
Option D: Anterior Uveitis Presents with ciliary congestion, keratic precipitates, synechiae; not dandruff-like scales.

stem: An elderly female presents with bullous keratopathy. Guttate lesions (endothelial dusting) are observed in the other eye. Diagnosis:
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
  • Fuch's endothelial dystrophy is a progressive bilateral corneal endothelial disease, usually in elderly women.
  • Characterized by endothelial guttata (beaten-metal appearance) and progressive endothelial cell loss.
  • Leads to stromal edema and bullous keratopathy due to fluid accumulation.
  • Ultimately, it causes painful epithelial bullae and reduced vision.


Other Options:
Option B: Keratoconus Thinning and ectasia of cornea; produces irregular astigmatism, not bullous keratopathy.
Option C: Band-shaped keratopathy Calcium deposition in Bowman's layer; appears as band opacity across cornea.
Option D: Endothelial graft failure Occurs after corneal transplant; a history of surgery would be present.

stem: A 15-year-old girl presents with a mass on the lateral canthus, slowly increasing in size, painless and without deviation of eye. Diagnosis:
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
  • Dermoid cyst is a congenital choristoma, commonly located at the lateral canthus (near zygomaticofrontal suture).
  • Features: slow-growing, painless, firm, non-tender mass; does not cause globe displacement.
  • Typically detected in childhood or adolescence.
  • Important because rupture may lead to inflammatory response.


Other Options:
  • Capillary hemangioma: It is common in infants, reddish compressible swelling, not a firm cystic mass.
  • Chalazion: Chronic granulomatous inflammation of the meibomian gland, usually on eyelid, not lateral canthus.
  • Neurofibroma: It is seen in NF-1; often multiple, associated with plexiform pattern and "S-shaped" eyelid.

stem: The image shows an intracorneal ring segment (ICRS/INTACS). It is used for management of which condition?
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
  • Intracorneal ring segments (ICRS/Intacs) are small PMMA implants placed in the corneal stroma.
  • They flatten the cornea, reduce irregular astigmatism and improve vision.
  • Indicated in keratoconus, especially when contact lens intolerance develops and corneal graft is not yet needed.
  • They do not halt disease progression (that is the role of corneal collagen cross-linking).


Other Options:
  • Corneal ulcer: Managed with antimicrobials, not rings.
  • Bullous keratopathy: Managed with keratoplasty or endothelial procedures.
  • Corneal dystrophy: Managed according to type (transplant often required).

stem: Which of the following is true about orbital cellulitis?
options: Ethmoidal sinusitis is a common cause
Topical antibiotics with steroids is the treatment
Presents with proptosis and normal ocular movements
It is limited by the orbital septum
subject: Ophthalmology
explanation:
Solution
  • Orbital cellulitis is an infection of orbital contents posterior to the orbital septum.
  • The most common source is extension from ethmoidal sinusitis due to the thin lamina papyracea.
  • Features: fever, proptosis, painful restricted ocular movements, chemosis and vision loss if the optic nerve is compromised.
  • Requires systemic antibiotics surgical drainage; topical therapy alone is ineffective.


Other Options:
  • Topical antibiotics with steroids: Systemic therapy is mandatory.
  • Proptosis with normal movements: It is seen in preseptal cellulitis, not orbital cellulitis.
  • Limited by orbital septum: Orbital cellulitis is posterior to septum, unlike preseptal cellulitis.

stem: Identify the structure marked with white arrow in the scan shown for the given patient:
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
  • The pyriform fossa (pyriform sinus) is a recess located on either side of the laryngeal inlet.
  • On imaging/endoscopy, it lies lateral to the aryepiglottic fold.
  • It is a common site for foreign body impaction, especially fish bones.
  • Cancers here are classified as hypopharyngeal carcinomas, often presenting late with neck nodes.
  • Recognition of pyriform sinus anatomy is crucial for ENT diagnosis and surgery.


Other Options:
Option A: Thyroid cartilage is a framework cartilage, not a mucosal recess.
Option B: Vallecula lies between base of tongue and epiglottis, not lateral to larynx.
Option D: Epiglottis is a cartilage guarding the inlet, not a recess.

stem: A 27-year-old male presents with complaints of reduced hearing in the left ear. Audiogram shows findings suggestive of:
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
  • In conductive hearing loss (CHL), air conduction thresholds are raised while bone conduction remains normal, producing an air-bone gap.
  • The audiogram shows this pattern in the left ear, confirming left-sided CHL.
  • Causes include CSOM, otosclerosis and tympanic membrane perforation.
  • CHL is usually correctable with medical or surgical treatment.


Other Options:
Option A: Right ear is normal.
Option C: Right ear SNHL would show elevated AC and BC in the right ear.
Option D: Left ear SNHL would show elevated AC and BC without a gap.

stem: A 55-year-old male with hypertension and T2DM presents with posterior epistaxis not responding to anterior nasal packing. Which artery should be ligated first?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
  • The sphenopalatine artery (branch of maxillary artery) is the major supply of the posterior nasal cavity.
  • Posterior epistaxis, common in elderly hypertensives, arises from this source.
  • If anterior packing fails, endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation is the procedure of choice.
  • This has replaced older external carotid artery ligation.


Other Options:
Option A: Anterior ethmoidal artery supplies anterior superior septum.
Option B: Posterior ethmoidal artery supplies a small superior area.
Option C: Greater palatine artery supplies inferior septum and hard palate.

stem: Identify the procedure done in this patient.
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
  • The image shows absence of larynx with permanent tracheostoma, typical of total laryngectomy.
  • Entire larynx, including vocal cords, is removed, usually for advanced laryngeal carcinoma.
  • The patient breathes through the stoma and cannot speak normally.
  • Rehabilitation includes tracheoesophageal prosthesis or esophageal speech.


Other Options:
Option B: Partial laryngectomy preserves part of the larynx.
Option C: Standard tracheostomy creates a temporary opening without removing the larynx.
Option D: Percutaneous tracheostomy is a bedside dilatational procedure.

stem: Which among the following is the nerve supply to the marked structures (Dark arrows)?
options: Nerve to mylohyoid and facial nerve
Nerve to mylohyoid and spinal accessory nerve
Hypoglossal nerve and spinal accessory nerve
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
  • The anterior belly of digastric is supplied by the nerve to mylohyoid (branch of mandibular nerve V3).
  • The posterior belly of digastric is supplied by the facial nerve.
  • This dual innervation is a key anatomical fact.
  • Both assist in mandibular depression and hyoid elevation during swallowing and speech.


Other Options:
Option B: Spinal accessory does not supply digastric.
Option C: Hypoglossal supplies tongue muscles, not digastric.
Option D: Combination is incorrect; mylohyoid is not supplied by hypoglossal.

stem: A 25-year-old male presents with nasal bone fracture after RTA. Most appropriate instrument for reduction?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
  • Walsham forceps are used for closed reduction of nasal bone fractures.
  • They have a rubber guard to prevent mucosal trauma.
  • Reduction is ideally performed within 7-10 days.


Other Options:
Option B: Killian's speculum is for septum visualization.
Option C: Asch forceps are used for septal fractures.
Option D: Luc's forceps are for polyp removal.

stem: A 28-year-old male with chronic ear discharge and hearing loss. Most common surgery done:
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
  • Chronic ear discharge with hearing loss most commonly suggests Chronic Suppurative Otitis Media (CSOM), especially the tubotympanic (safe) type.
  • The standard surgical treatment for CSOM (safe type) is tympanoplasty.
  • Tympanoplasty involves repair of the tympanic membrane with or without ossicular reconstruction.
  • It helps in eradication of disease and restoration of hearing. It is the most commonly performed ear surgery for chronic ear discharge.
  • Myringoplasty is a type of tympanoplasty limited to membrane repair, but tympanoplasty is the broader and more commonly indicated procedure.


Other Options:
Option A: Atticotomy. Done mainly for attic cholesteatoma. Not indicated in routine safe-type CSOM. Less commonly performed compared to tympanoplasty.
Option B: Modified radical mastoidectomy. Indicated in unsafe CSOM with cholesteatoma. A more extensive surgery aimed at disease clearance. Not the most common surgery for simple chronic discharge.
Option D: Myringoplasty. Involves only closure of tympanic membrane perforation. Does not address ossicular pathology if present. Tympanoplasty is more comprehensive and more commonly performed overall.

stem: A patient presents with hearing loss. Rinne's test negative on left and positive on right; Weber's test lateralizes to left. Diagnosis:
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
  • Rinne negative on the left = bone > air = CHL on the left side.
  • Weber lateralizes left = CHL on left side.
  • Together, this confirms the left CHL.


Other Options:
Option A: Right ear normal.
Option C: Right SNHL would lateralize Weber to right.
Option D: Left SNHL would lateralize Weber to the opposite ear.

stem: A patient in his 20s presents with nasal obstruction and recurrent epistaxis. Nasal endoscopy shows a red mass. Likely diagnosis?
subject: ENT
explanation:
Solution
  • Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA) is a benign vascular tumor in adolescent males.
  • Presents with nasal obstruction + recurrent severe epistaxis.
  • Endoscopy shows a red mass in the nasopharynx/nasal cavity.
  • Biopsy is avoided due to risk of profuse bleeding; imaging confirms.
  • Management = surgical excision after embolization.


Other Options:
Option B: AC polyp is pale, not vascular.
Option C: Ethmoidal polyps are multiple, bilateral, non-bleeding.
Option D: Inverted papilloma occurs in older adults with unilateral obstruction, not heavy bleeding.

stem: A patient scheduled for surgery develops preoperative bradycardia, hypotension and facial flushing. Which of the following muscle relaxants is most likely responsible for these symptoms?
subject: Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
  • Atracurium can cause histamine release, leading to bradycardia, hypotension and flushing.
  • It is an intermediate-acting non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocker.
  • The histamine release is dose-dependent.
  • Cisatracurium (a stereoisomer) is safer and does not release histamine.


Other Options:
Option B: CisAtracurium is free from histamine release.
Option C: Pancuronium causes tachycardia (vagolytic), not bradycardia.
Option D: Vecuronium is cardiovascularly stable.

stem: A patient undergoing abdominal surgery under GA is administered succinylcholine along with sevoflurane. Shortly after, the patient develops muscle rigidity, increase in temperature and increase in CO2 levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate life-saving drug in this scenario?
subject: Anesthesia
explanation:
Solution
  • Malignant hyperthermia is triggered by succinylcholine + volatile anesthetics (e.g. sevoflurane).
  • Clinical triad: muscle rigidity, hyperthermia, hypercapnia.
  • The antidote is Dantrolene, which blocks calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.


Other Options:
Option B: Diazepam treats spasms but not MH.
Option C: Propofol is an anesthetic, not an antidote.
Option D: Intralipid is used in local anesthetic toxicity, not MH.

stem: A 48-year-old man with COVID-19 is admitted in the ICU and is currently on mechanical ventilation. The PaO2/FiO2 ratio is 100, indicating severe ARDS. Which of the following is the most appropriate ventilatory strategy to manage this patient?
options: High PEEP and low tidal volume
High PEEP and high tidal volume
Low PEEP and low tidal volume
Low PEEP and high tidal volume
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Severe ARDS management includes:
  • 1. Low tidal volume ventilation (6 mL/kg) - prevents volutrauma.
  • 2. High PEEP → prevents alveolar collapse, improves oxygenation.
  • PaO2/FiO2 < 100 = severe ARDS.


Other Options:
Option B: High tidal volume causes barotrauma.
Option C: Low PEEP worsens hypoxemia.
Option D: High tidal volume + low PEEP is harmful.

stem: Following a building collapse, a patient presents to the casualty with airway obstruction. The procedure shown in the given image is performed on the patient. Which of the following statements regarding the procedure is true?
options: It provides adequate ventilation for up to 72 hours
It must be followed by formal tracheostomy
It allows for removal of large foreign bodies from the airway
It can be safely used for prolonged airway management without further intervention
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • The procedure = cricothyrotomy (emergency airway).
  • Provides a temporary airway in cases of obstruction.
  • Must be followed by formal tracheostomy for long-term management.


Other Options:
Option A: Not safe beyond a few hours.
Option C: Does not allow removal of large foreign bodies.
Option D: Unsafe for prolonged use due to complications (subglottic stenosis).

stem: A patient presented with itchy rash on elbows, buttocks and a recent diagnosis of gluten sensitive enteropathy. Immunofluorescence shows IgA deposition. Most likely diagnosis:
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
  • Dermatitis herpetiformis is closely linked to gluten-sensitive enteropathy (celiac disease).
  • Direct immunofluorescence demonstrates granular IgA deposition at dermal papillae.
  • Presents with intensely pruritic vesicles on extensor surfaces (elbows, knees, buttocks).
  • Rash often occurs in young to middle-aged adults.
  • Responds well to dapsone and a gluten-free diet.


Other Options:
Option B: Shows linear IgA at BMZ, not granular.
Option C: IgG at BMZ; tense bullae in elderly.
Option D: IgG against desmoglein-3, with intraepidermal blistering and oral mucosa involvement.

stem: A patient has freckles, severe photosensitivity and basal cell carcinoma. Which DNA repair mechanism is most likely impaired?
subject: Biochemistry
explanation:
Solution
  • Features suggest Xeroderma pigmentosum (XP).
  • It is caused by defective nucleotide excision repair (NER).
  • NER removes UV-induced thymine dimers.
  • Defect → DNA damage accumulation → early skin cancers (BCC, SCC, melanoma).
  • Patients present with severe photosensitivity, freckles, ocular involvement, neurological degeneration.


Other Options:
Option A: Defective in HNPCC (Lynch syndrome).
Option C: Repairs double-strand breaks; defective in immunodeficiency.
Option D: Repairs small base damage (oxidation/alkylation).

stem: 20-year-old with stable hypopigmented patch since birth. Wood's lamp shows accentuated whiteness; diascopy negative. Most likely diagnosis:
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
  • Nevus depigmentosus is a congenital, non-progressive hypopigmented lesion.
  • Wood's lamp: off-white accentuation (not chalk-white like vitiligo).
  • Diascopy negative, confirming non-vascular cause.
  • Lesion remains stable throughout life.
  • No systemic associations.


Other Options:
Option A: Autoimmune, progressive depigmentation.
Option C: It is due to vascular abnormality, lesion fades with diascopy.
Option D: Hypopigmented patch with sensory loss and nerve thickening.

stem: A patient presents with a swollen ankle, discharging sinuses, black granules. Likely finding in KOH?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Clinical features: Madura foot (mycetoma) with black granules.
  • KOH mount shows slender dematiaceous fungi in eumycetoma.
  • Causative organisms: Madurella mycetomatis.
  • Disease is chronic, deforming and progressive.
  • Leads to sinus formation and discharge.


Other Options:
Option A: It is seen in dermatophytes.
Option C: It is typical of Candida infection.
Option D: It is seen in mucormycosis.

stem: Young adult with facial pain, painful oral vesicles. Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells with inclusion bodies. Most probable pathogen?
subject: Microbiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Herpes simplex virus (HSV) → painful grouped vesicles on erythematous base.
  • Tzanck smear shows multinucleated giant cells with intranuclear inclusions.
  • Usually HSV-1 (oral lesions) or HSV-2 (genital).
  • Lesions are recurrent, triggered by fever/stress.
  • Confirmed by PCR/viral culture.


Other Options:
Option A: It causes infectious mononucleosis.
Option B: It shows owl's eye inclusions.
Option C: It causes warts, not vesicles.

stem: A patient presented with indurated plaque on cheek with central atrophy and apical calcification on chest X-ray. Most appropriate test to confirm diagnosis?
subject: Dermatology
explanation:
Solution
  • Clinical features suggest cutaneous tuberculosis (lupus vulgaris).
  • Apical calcification on chest X-ray indicates TB association.
  • PCR detects M. tuberculosis DNA → most rapid and specific test.
  • More reliable than Mantoux or culture (slow).
  • Important for early diagnosis and management.


Other Options:
Option A: It shows exposure, not confirmation.
Option B: It is used for leprosy, not TB.
Option D: It is used for leprosy nerve involvement, not TB.

stem: A 30-year-old patient presents to the clinic 2 weeks after witnessing his father's sudden death. He reports nightmares, intrusive flashbacks and hypervigilance. These symptoms cause significant distress but have been present for less than 1 month. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
  • Acute Stress Disorder (ASD) occurs within 3 days to 1 month following a traumatic event.
  • Symptoms include intrusive memories, nightmares, flashbacks and hypervigilance.
  • Key differentiating factor from PTSD: duration < 1 month.
  • PTSD is diagnosed only when symptoms persist >1 month.
  • Early identification and intervention can reduce risk of progression to PTSD.


Other Options:
Option A: It requires symptoms >1 month.
Option C: Emotional/behavioral symptoms after stress but not trauma-specific.
Option D: It is characterized by excessive worry across domains, not trauma-specific.

stem: A patient's friend describes Mr. K as being rigid, overly focused on rules and order and a perfectionist. He often submits work late because he insists on making it perfect and believes strongly in high moral principles. Which of the following is the most likely personality disorder?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
  • Obsessive-Compulsive Personality Disorder (OCPD) is a Cluster C personality disorder.
  • Characterized by perfectionism, rigidity and excessive devotion to work and rules.
  • Individuals often miss deadlines due to striving for unattainable perfection.
  • They have inflexible moral/ethical standards.
  • Unlike OCD, OCPD does not involve intrusive thoughts or compulsions.


Other Options:
Option B: It is characterized by grandiosity and lack of empathy, not perfectionism.
Option C: Features include suspiciousness and mistrust.
Option D: It is characterized by social inhibition and hypersensitivity to rejection.

stem: A 35-year-old man is found wandering in another city by a social worker. When questioned, he appears confused about his identity and cannot recall how he arrived there. He has no signs of intoxication or head injury and medical evaluation is normal. He is unable to provide personal information and seems to have sudden, unexpected travel away from home. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
  • Dissociative Fugue involves sudden unexpected travel away from home with inability to recall one's past or identity.
  • Patients may appear confused or assume a new identity.
  • Often associated with stressful or traumatic life events.
  • Differentiated from amnesia by associated travel/relocation behavior.
  • Usually resolves spontaneously but may recur with stress.


Other Options:
Option A: It is memory loss without travel.
Option C: It is acute, short-lived anterograde amnesia in middle-aged/elderly.
Option D: It is psychogenic events resembling seizures; no fugue state.

stem: A patient with a known history of mental illness presents after murdering another individual. He reports having paranoid delusions that the person was plotting against him, which drove him to commit the act. What is the next best step in the legal and clinical management of this patient?
options: The patient is still guilty of murder
Refer the patient to psychiatry for comprehensive mental health evaluation
Automatically consider him mentally ill and declare him not guilty
Proceed with routine trial without psychiatric involvement
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
  • Patients with severe mental illness and delusions may commit crimes under impaired judgment.
  • The next step is referral for comprehensive psychiatric evaluation.
  • Psychiatry will assess criminal responsibility under the McNaughten rule (insanity defense).
  • Courts require expert psychiatric input before passing judgment.
  • Directly declaring guilt or innocence is not appropriate without evaluation.


Other Options:
Option A: Declaring guilty without psychiatric evaluation ignores mental illness factors.
Option C: Automatically declaring not guilty is inappropriate without assessment.
Option D: Proceeding with routine trials disregards psychiatric contribution.

stem: A young man presents to the psychiatry OPD reporting that whenever he sits down, he hears birds flapping their wings. He believes this is a sign from God directing him to move in a specific direction to stay safe. He also describes that when he goes out, birds chirping is another way God communicates with him. Which of the following psychopathological phenomena does this scenario best represent?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
  • Delusional perception: A normal perception is given a delusional meaning.
  • Example: birds chirping interpreted as divine communication.
  • It is one of the first-rank symptoms of schizophrenia.
  • Seen in early stages of psychosis.
  • Represents a bridge between perception and delusion.


Other Options:
Option A: It appears abruptly without any perceptual trigger.
Option C: It is delusions of persecution and is not perception-linked.
Option D: It is a belief that external agents insert thoughts into one's mind.

stem: A schizophrenia patient on haloperidol was responding well for the last 2 years. Now presented with orofacial dyskinesia, choreiform, tic-like movements and possible dystonia. What can be the probable diagnosis and treatment?
subject: Psychiatry
explanation:
Solution
  • Tardive dyskinesia: Late-onset hyperkinetic movement disorder from long-term antipsychotic use.
  • Movements: orofacial dyskinesia, choreiform and tic-like movements.
  • Pathophysiology: dopamine receptor supersensitivity in basal ganglia.
  • Valbenazine (VMAT2 inhibitor) is an FDA-approved treatment.
  • Early recognition is crucial to prevent progression.


Other Options:
Option A: It occurs early, treated with anticholinergics (benztropine), not ropinirole.
Option C: Inner restlessness, best treated with propranolol.
Option D: It is seen in drug-induced parkinsonism, treated with amantadine/anticholinergics.

stem: Which of the following statements is true regarding the procedure shown (IVP)?
options: Invasive method to diagnose bladder cancer
Non-invasive method to diagnose PUJ obstruction
Can be used in percutaneous guided procedure
Invasive method to diagnose bladder stone
subject: Radiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Intravenous Pyelogram (IVP) is a non-invasive imaging study using IV contrast to outlines kidneys, ureters and bladder.
  • Useful in diagnosing pelvi-ureteric junction (PUJ) obstruction and urinary tract anomalies.
  • Provides both anatomical and functional information.
  • Has largely been replaced by ultrasound and CT urography, but still important in exams.
  • No direct role in diagnosing bladder cancer or stones invasively.


Other Options:
Option A: Not invasive; bladder cancer is usually diagnosed by cystoscopy + biopsy.
Option C: IVP is diagnostic only; not used in guided procedures.
Option D: Bladder stones are best diagnosed by ultrasound/CT.

stem: History of pulsatile mass in neck. Digital angiography image shows refilling on release of compression. Diagnosis:
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Carotid body tumors (chemodectomas/paragangliomas) are highly vascular tumors.
  • Classic feature: "Lyre sign" on angiography (splaying of carotid bifurcation).
  • Tumor shows refilling after compression release due to rich vascular supply.
  • Clinically, it presents as a pulsatile lateral neck mass.
  • Confirmed with CT/MRI angiography.


Other Options:
Option A: It is expansile vascular dilatation, not solid mass.
Option C: It is an abnormal vascular connection that causes continuous bruit, not focal tumor.
Option D: It is a benign vascular malformation, usually cutaneous/subcutaneous.

stem: 50-year-old male with a headache. MRI brain shows dural-based homogeneously enhancing extra-axial lesion with dural tail. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Radiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Meningioma is the most common extra-axial intracranial tumor.
  • Imaging hallmark: dural-based lesion with homogeneous enhancement.
  • "Dural tail sign" on MRI is characteristic.
  • Usually benign and slow-growing.
  • More common in middle-aged women.


Other Options:
Option B: It is an intra-axial, infiltrative tumor.
Option C: It arises in ventricles, especially the 4th ventricle in children.
Option D: It is a benign pediatric tumor, cystic with a mural nodule.

stem: 35-year-old female with lower abdominal discomfort. MRI shown. Diagnosis:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Adenomyosis is the presence of endometrial glands and stroma within the myometrium.
  • MRI shows diffuse uterine enlargement, ill-defined junctional zone thickening (>12 mm).
  • Appears as low-signal intensity areas on T2-weighted MRI due to smooth muscle hyperplasia.
  • Symptoms: dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, chronic pelvic pain.
  • Often seen in multiparous women in reproductive age.


Other Options:
Option A: It is a well-circumscribed, round, hypointense lesion on MRI.
Option B: It is seen as chocolate cysts/endometriomas, not diffuse uterine thickening.
Option D: It is a focal lesion in the endometrial cavity, best seen on hysterosonography.

stem: Neonate diagnosed with congenital hypothyroidism. Thyroid ultrasound shows normal gland in-situ, but radioiodine uptake scan shows no uptake. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • In congenital hypothyroidism, thyroid scintigraphy helps distinguish causes.
  • Normal thyroid gland in-situ with absent uptake suggests defective iodide transporter/receptor (thyroid iodide symporter defect).
  • This prevents iodide from being utilized despite gland presence.
  • It presents as hypothyroidism at birth, requiring lifelong thyroxine therapy.
  • Differentiates from agenesis (no gland visualized).


Other Options:
Option A: US would show absent gland, not normal gland.
Option B: Uptake is present but hormone synthesis is defective.
Option C: Uptake is usually increased, not absent.

stem: Right flank pain. CT image shown. Likely diagnosis:
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Renal cell carcinoma (RCC) is the most common renal malignancy.
  • CT shows enhancing mass in the renal parenchyma (heterogeneous due to necrosis/hemorrhage).
  • Classical triad: hematuria, flank pain, abdominal mass (but uncommon together).
  • RCC may invade renal vein/IVC.
  • CT is the gold standard for staging and surgical planning.


Other Options:
Option A: It is typically separate from the kidney.
Option B: It is localized in RUQ, not renal fossa.
Option D: It is a lesion in the liver, not the kidney.

stem: 8-year-old child with painless limp. X-ray pelvis shown. Diagnosis:
subject: Orthopedics
explanation:
Solution
  • Perthes disease is an idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head in children (4-10 years).
  • X-ray: fragmentation, sclerosis and collapse of femoral head.
  • It presents with painless limp and limited abduction/internal rotation.
  • It is more common in boys, unilateral in 90%.
  • Early diagnosis is critical to prevent deformity and arthritis.


Other Options:
Option B: It is painful arthritis with joint space narrowing, not painless limp.
Option C: It occurs in adolescents, femoral head slips posteriorly.
Option D: It is present since infancy with abnormal hip development.

stem: Neonate presents with respiratory distress soon after birth. CXR shows bowel loops in thorax. Diagnosis:
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH) is herniation of abdominal viscera into the thorax.
  • The most common type includes Bochdalek hernia (posterolateral, left-sided).
  • CXR: bowel loops in thorax with mediastinal shift.
  • It presents with respiratory distress immediately after birth.
  • Requires urgent ventilatory support and surgical repair.


Other Options:
Option B: It is rare in neonates, not associated with bowel loops.
Option C: It shows air and not bowel pattern.
Option D: It shows diffuse ground-glass lungs, no herniated bowel.

stem: A 10-year-old child presents with headache, visual field defects and growth disturbances. MRI shows a suprasellar cystic mass. Histology reveals stratified squamous epithelium with wet keratin. Diagnosis:
subject: Pathology
explanation:
Solution
  • Craniopharyngioma is a benign suprasellar tumor from Rathke's pouch remnants.
  • MRI shows suprasellar cystic + solid mass, often calcified.
  • Histology showing stratified squamous epithelium with wet keratin is diagnostic.
  • Clinical features include headache, bitemporal hemianopia, growth hormone deficiency.
  • Requires surgical excision ± radiotherapy.


Other Options:
Option A: It is purely sellar, lacks keratin histology.
Option B: It is cystic but lined by cuboidal/columnar epithelium.
Option C: It is an intrinsic brain tumor with no epithelial lining.

stem: A 32-year-old woman with migraine was started on sumatriptan. She has no comorbidities but has a positive family history of coronary artery disease. After the first dose, she developed chest tightness. She is now planned for migraine prophylaxis. Which of the following is an appropriate drug for migraine prophylaxis in this patient?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Propranolol, a non-selective β-blocker, is a first-line agent for migraine prophylaxis.
  • It reduces the frequency and severity of migraine attacks.
  • Sumatriptan caused chest tightness in this patient, so prophylaxis is indicated.
  • Other agents like Topiramate or Flunarizine are alternatives but propranolol is preferred unless contraindicated.


Other Options:
Option A: Topiramate is effective but usually second-line.
Option C: Naratriptan is abortive, not prophylactic.
Option D: Flunarizine can be used but is less preferred.

stem: A 36-year-old woman with a history of severe eosinophilic asthma continues to experience frequent exacerbations despite being on high-dose inhaled corticosteroids and long-acting beta-agonists. Her blood eosinophil count is significantly elevated. The pulmonologist decides to start her on benralizumab as an add-on therapy. Benralizumab acts by targeting which of the following interleukins?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Benralizumab is a monoclonal antibody that targets IL-5 receptor α.
  • IL-5 is crucial for the growth, differentiation and survival of eosinophils.
  • Blocking IL-5 leads to eosinophil depletion, improving control in eosinophilic asthma.
  • Other biologics: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5), Dupilumab (anti-IL-4/IL-13).


Other Options:
Option A: IL-1 inhibitors (anakinra) used in autoinflammatory syndromes.
Option B: IL-4 targeted by dupilumab.
Option C: IL-6 targeted by tocilizumab (RA, cytokine storm).

stem: A 45-year-old female presented with a 6-month history of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, palpitations and breathlessness. On evaluation: BP normal, SpO2 normal, JVP elevated, irregularly irregular pulse, tender hepatomegaly, heaving apex, mid-diastolic murmur heard at the apex. This patient also has a history of acute rheumatic fever. Which of the following is false?
options: This patient has increased risk of stroke
Patient has features of right heart failure
Presystolic accentuation of MDM is expected to be hallmark feature in this case
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • This patient has mitral stenosis with atrial fibrillation (irregularly irregular pulse, mid-diastolic murmur, rheumatic history).
  • AF causes loss of atrial contraction, so no presystolic accentuation of the murmur is heard.
  • Stroke risk is increased due to left atrial thrombus formation.
  • Elevated JVP and hepatomegaly = right heart failure due to pulmonary hypertension.


Other Options:
Option A: AF + MS increases stroke risk.
Option B: AF causes absent a wave.
Option C: Right heart failure features present.

stem: A 56-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus is on metformin. His HbA1c is 8.2% on current therapy. He is now diagnosed with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF). Which of the following is the most appropriate add-on therapy?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Empagliflozin, an SGLT-2 inhibitor, improves both glycemic control and outcomes in patients with HFrEF.
  • Reduces hospitalization and cardiovascular mortality.
  • Pioglitazone is contraindicated in HF (causes fluid retention).


Other Options:
Option B: Sitagliptin helps glycemic control but no proven HF benefit.
Option C: Glimepiride may worsen hypoglycemia and weight gain.
Option D: Pioglitazone contraindicated in HFrEF.

stem: A case of atrial fibrillation presents with a stroke within 2 hours of onset. CT brain showed no bleed. What is the next best step in management?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Patient presents with acute ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours → eligible for thrombolysis with rtPA.
  • CT excludes hemorrhage, making thrombolysis safe.
  • Mechanical thrombectomy is considered in large vessel occlusion within 6 hours.
  • Anticoagulation is not an acute intervention.


Other Options:
Option B: Angiography is diagnostic, not the first step here.
Option C: Thrombectomy is reserved for selected large vessel cases.
Option D: Warfarin is for long-term prevention, not acute stroke.

stem: 15-year-old presented with breathing difficulty. Height 6.4 feet, enlarged jaw. Which finding confirms the diagnosis?
options: Increased IGF1, Suppressed GH after glucose
Increased IGF1, Non-suppressed GH after glucose
subject: Endocrinology
explanation:
Solution
  • Tall stature + enlarged jaw = acromegaly/gigantism.
  • Diagnostic hallmark = ↑ IGF-1 + failure of GH suppression after oral glucose tolerance test.
  • IGF-1 reflects long-term GH activity.


Other Options:
Option A: Decreased IGF-1 excludes diagnosis.
Option B: Increased IGF-1 alone not confirmatory.
Option C: GH is not suppressed here, so C is incorrect.

stem: 68-year-old CKD patient admitted to ICU with: Calcium - 7.2, Phosphate - 6.3, ALP-370, elevated PTH. How do you manage?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • CKD leads to secondary hyperparathyroidism due to hypocalcemia + hyperphosphatemia.
  • Best initial management: oral calcium + phosphate binders.
  • Vitamin D analogs can be considered later but risk hyperphosphatemia.


Other Options:
Option A: Binder alone is inadequate.
Option B: Calcium alone doesn't correct phosphate.
Option D: Vit D3 worsens phosphate retention.

stem: Which of the following is the most common chronic manifestation of Sjögren's syndrome?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Sjögren's syndrome is characterized by autoimmune destruction of exocrine glands.
  • The most common chronic manifestation is chronic lacrimal gland inflammation → keratoconjunctivitis sicca (dry eyes).
  • Associated with xerostomia and parotid enlargement.


Other Options:
Option A: Arthritis may occur but not most common.
Option C: RTA is rare.
Option D: ILD is a less common complication.

stem: Which of the following is most appropriate for prophylaxis of PSVT in a stable patient with a history of asthma?
subject: Pharmacology
explanation:
Solution
  • Verapamil (a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker) is effective for prophylaxis of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia (PSVT).
  • In asthma, β-blockers (like metoprolol) are avoided due to risk of bronchospasm.
  • Adenosine is used for acute termination, not prophylaxis.


Other Options:
Option A: Adenosine is acute therapy, not for prophylaxis.
Option B: Amiodarone is not first-line due to toxicity.
Option D: Metoprolol is contraindicated in asthma.

stem: A young patient presents with a history of atrial fibrillation (AF) and no evidence of thromboembolic risk factors. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: No anticoagulation needed at this stage
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Stroke risk in AF is assessed with the CHA2DS2-VASc score.
  • Young patient with no risk factors → score = 0.
  • No anticoagulation is needed in such patients.


Other Options:
Option A: Anticoagulants needed if score ≥2.
Option B: Aspirin no longer recommended for stroke prevention in AF.
Option D: Dual antiplatelet not standard.

stem: A 21-year-old female with IDDM presents with altered sensorium. History of missed insulin doses. BP 80/60 mmHg, HR 103/min. Labs: Na: 126 mEq/L, K: 4.3 mEq/L, BUN: 79 mg/dL. Creatinine: 2.2 mg/dL, RBS: 720 mg/dL. What is not part of initial management?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • The patient is in diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) with hypotension and altered sensorium.
  • Initial management = IV fluids (0.9% NaCl), insulin and potassium monitoring.
  • 3% NaCl (hypertonic saline) is not used → only in severe symptomatic hyponatremia.


Other Options:
Option B: Correct first step for volume resuscitation.
Option C: Insulin is essential.
Option D: K monitoring prevents arrhythmias.

stem: A patient develops acute quadriparesis after rapid correction of hyponatremia. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Rapid correction of hyponatremia → osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis).
  • Presents with quadriparesis, pseudobulbar palsy, altered consciousness.
  • MRI brain shows demyelination in pons.


Other Options:
Option A: Evoked potentials not diagnostic.
Option B: EEG shows nonspecific slowing.
Option D: NCS does not detect central demyelination.

stem: 24-year-old male patient with progressive low back pain for 1 year, morning stiffness and red eye. X-ray of sacroiliac (SI) joint is normal. Best test for early diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Symptoms: back pain + morning stiffness + uveitis = ankylosing spondylitis.
  • Early stage: X-ray may be normal.
  • MRI detects sacroiliitis early → gold standard for early diagnosis.


Other Options:
Option A: CT not sensitive for early inflammation.
Option C: Anti-CCP is for RA, not AS.
Option D: LS spine X-ray detects later changes.

stem: 45-year-old male with cough, low-grade fever. Chest X-ray shown. Most likely diagnosis:
subject: Radiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Clinical features + chest X-ray opacity in RUL = consolidation.
  • Commonly bacterial pneumonia (Streptococcus pneumoniae).
  • Collapse shows volume loss, shift; abscess shows cavity; malignancy shows irregular mass.


Other Options:
Option B: Collapse shows tracheal shift.
Option C: Malignancy unlikely with acute fever.
Option D: Abscess shows cavity with air-fluid level.

stem: A 25-year-old male had RTA, later became unconscious and died. Multiple petechial hemorrhages in corpus callosum were found. Diagnosis?
subject: Forensic Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • Diffuse axonal injury (DAI) = severe head trauma → shearing injury to axons.
  • Pathology: multiple petechial hemorrhages in corpus callosum, brainstem, gray-white junction.
  • Presents with coma after trauma, often without large hematoma.


Other Options:
Option A: EDH = biconvex hematoma, lucid interval.
Option C: SDH = crescent-shaped hematoma.
Option D: Ischemic injury shows infarcts, not hemorrhages.

stem: A patient presents with breast cancer. The clinical image is given, there is no evidence of chest wall fixation or inflammatory changes. Based on AJCC-TNM staging, what is the most appropriate T-stage?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • T4d = Inflammatory carcinoma in AJCC breast cancer staging.
  • Characterized by diffuse erythema, edema (peau d'orange), and rapid progression.
  • Even without chest wall fixation, inflammatory changes elevate staging to T4d.
  • Prognosis is poor; multimodal treatment required.


Other Options:
Option A: T3 = tumor >5 cm without chest wall or skin involvement.
Option B: T4b = ulceration or satellite nodules on skin.
Option C: T4c = both T4a (chest wall) and T4b (skin).

stem: Following is the image of barium swallow suggestive of LES constriction. What will be the investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Achalasia cardia shows "bird beak" narrowing on barium swallow.
  • Gold standard diagnostic test of Esophageal manometry, showing absent peristalsis + incomplete LES relaxation.
  • Endoscopy rules out malignancy but is not diagnostic.


Other Options:
Option A: CECT is not diagnostic for achalasia.
Option B: Endoscopy rules out obstruction but not confirmatory.
Option D: TEE evaluates heart, not esophagus motility.

stem: A patient presented with a stab wound to the abdomen. On examination there was hypotension with abdominal wall tenderness suggestive of peritonitis. What is the next appropriate step?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Hemodynamically unstable patient with penetrating abdominal trauma + peritonitis → immediate exploratory laparotomy is indicated.
  • No time for imaging or diagnostic procedures.
  • FAST/DPL useful only if diagnosis is uncertain or patient stable.


Other Options:
Option B: FAST is initial screen but not in unstable peritonitis.
Option C: DPL is historical, rarely used now.
Option D: CECT requires stability, contraindicated in unstable patients.

stem: A 60-year-old patient presented to OPD with jaundice. On examination, there were scratch marks over the skin surface and a palpable gall bladder which was non-tender. What is the likely diagnosis?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Courvoisier's law: Painless progressive jaundice + palpable non-tender gall bladder = malignant obstruction (commonly pancreatic head carcinoma).
  • The obstruction causes cholestatic jaundice and pruritus (scratch marks).
  • Choledocholithiasis usually presents with painful jaundice.


Other Options:
Option A: HCC presents with liver mass, not Courvoisier's sign.
Option B: Stones cause painful, not painless, jaundice.
Option D: Mucocele = distended gall bladder without jaundice.

stem: A patient presented with anal cancer below the dentate line. Which is the most common lymph node involved?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
  • Anal canal below the dentate line drains into the superficial inguinal lymph nodes.
  • Above the dentate line drains into internal iliac nodes.
  • Lymphatic spread determines staging and prognosis in anal cancer.


Other Options:
Option B: Deep inguinal less commonly involved.
Option C: Both not typical; site-specific drainage applies.
Option D: External iliac nodes drain pelvic organs, not anal canal below the dentate.

stem: A patient presented with abdominal pain. On examination it is associated with liver enlargement. Biopsy is obtained from the lesion and a CECT image is given. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Radiology
explanation:
Solution
  • Shows arterial phase hyperenhancement due to predominant hepatic arterial blood supply.
  • Demonstrates a characteristic heterogeneous "mosaic" pattern in large tumors.
  • Commonly associated with hepatomegaly and abdominal pain.
  • May show features of aggressive growth and vascular invasion on imaging.
  • Typical primary malignant tumor of the liver, especially in symptomatic patients.


Other Options:
Option A (Focal nodular hyperplasia): Usually an incidental finding in asymptomatic patients. Shows a central stellate scar on imaging. Does not exhibit arterial hyperenhancement with mosaic pattern.
Option B (Hepatic adenoma): Often linked to oral contraceptive use. Risk of hemorrhage, but lacks vascular invasion. Imaging does not show classic arterial hyperenhancement with washout.
Option C (Hemangioma of liver): Shows peripheral nodular enhancement with centripetal fill-in. Typically benign and well-circumscribed. Does not demonstrate mosaic pattern or malignant features.

stem: According to Parks classification, how would you classify the following fistula in ano?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Parks classification is based on sphincter involvement.
  • Intersphincteric fistula runs between internal and external sphincters.
  • It is the most common type (~70%).
  • High-yield: Intersphincteric = the most common type.


Other Options:
Option B: Transsphincteric passes through both sphincters.
Option C: Suprasphincteric loops above external sphincter.
Option D: Extrasphincteric bypasses sphincters entirely.

stem: Which of the following statement is true regarding the suture material given?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Monofilament sutures are made of a single strand.
  • Advantages: less tissue drag, lower infection risk.
  • Disadvantages: higher memory, poorer knot security.


Other Options:
Option B: Beaded not a standard suture property.
Option C: Can be absorbable or non-absorbable.
Option D: Actually high memory, not low.

stem: A patient develops carpopedal spasm on the second day following a total thyroidectomy. What is the most likely cause of this complication?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcemia is due to accidental removal or devascularization of parathyroid glands.
  • Leads to hypoparathyroidism → PTH hypocalcemia.
  • Clinical signs: carpopedal spasm, Chvostek's and Trousseau's signs, tetany.


Other Options:
Option A: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury → hoarseness, not tetany.
Option B: Thyroid storm → fever, tachycardia, restlessness.
Option D: Hypothyroidism develops weeks-months later, not immediate.

stem: A patient with circumferential full-thickness burns over the right lower limb is developing progressive pain, pallor, and decreased distal pulses. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Elevate the limb and apply ice packs
Start IV antibiotics and wait for slough separation
Perform escharotomy to relieve vascular compromise
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Circumferential full-thickness burns form a rigid eschar acts like a tourniquet.
  • Causes vascular compromise (compartment syndrome) in underlying tissues.
  • Escharotomy involves linear cuts through eschar to restore perfusion.


Other Options:
Option A: Ice packs worsen ischemia.
Option B: Waiting risks permanent ischemia.
Option D: Skin grafting is done later after the wound bed is prepared.

stem: During a surgical procedure, a knot is tied using a double throw first followed by single throws, which provides extra friction and security in slippery tissues. Which type of knot is being described?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Surgeon's knot: double throw first, followed by single throws.
  • Provides extra friction → useful in slippery tissues (e.g., bowel, fascia).
  • Prevents loosening before the second throw is placed.
  • High-yield: Surgeon's knot = double throw start.


Other Options:
Option A: Reef knot = square knot, no double throw.
Option B: Granny knot = insecure, slips easily.
Option D: Square knot = secure, but less friction than surgeon's knot.

stem: During laparoscopic cholecystectomy, a bile duct injury occurs. The image shows leakage from a small duct in the liver bed (usually an accessory or segmental duct) without injury to the main hepatic or common bile ducts. According to the Strasberg classification, what type of bile duct injury is this?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Strasberg classification describes bile duct injuries during cholecystectomy.
  • Type A: leakage from minor ducts (cystic duct stump or accessory ducts).
  • Usually managed conservatively or with stenting.


Other Options:
Option B: Occlusion of part of biliary tree.
Option C: Transection without communication to main ducts.
Option D: Lateral injury to major ducts.

stem: A patient presents with dilated, tortuous superficial veins, consistent with varicose veins in his lower limbs. Which of the following statements is correct regarding this condition?
options: Varicose veins are only cosmetic and have no complications
Varicose veins can lead to eczema and pigmentation
Sclerotherapy is the treatment of choice for all cases
Surgery is contraindicated in symptomatic varicose veins
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Varicose veins cause venous hypertension → skin changes.
  • Complications: eczema, pigmentation, ulceration, lipodermatosclerosis.
  • Treatment depends on severity: compression, sclerotherapy, or surgery.


Other Options:
Option A: They do have complications.
Option C: Sclerotherapy is used in selected cases, not all.
Option D: Surgery is indicated in symptomatic cases.

stem: A 2-year-old child is brought to the emergency department in an unconscious state. There is no history of trauma. What is the first step in emergency management?
options: Secure the airway and assess breathing
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • Emergency management follows ABCs: Airway → Breathing → Circulation.
  • First priority in an unconscious child = secure airway and assess breathing.
  • IV glucose is important but comes later (after stabilization).
  • High-yield: Always start with Airway in pediatric emergencies.


Other Options:
Option A: Hypoglycemia may cause unconsciousness but the airway takes priority.
Option C: CPR is indicated only if there is no pulse.
Option D: LP not an emergency priority.

stem: There are linear incisions on the limbs of a burn patient. These incisions are along the medial and lateral aspects of the limb. Which of the following statements is true regarding the procedure?
options: This is used for circumferential burns to relieve pressure
This shows excision and skin grafting
The incisions are made to avoid visible blood vessels
The incisions are cosmetic and not clinically significant
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • Escharotomy involves linear cuts in eschar from circumferential burns.
  • Relieves pressure, restores circulation, prevents ischemia.
  • Done along medial & lateral aspects of limbs, avoiding neurovascular bundles.


Other Options:
Option B: Excision + grafting is a different procedure.
Option C: Not for avoiding vessels, but to relieve vascular compromise.
Option D: It is highly significant, not cosmetic.

stem: A 30-year-old man underwent knee surgery 7 days ago. He now presents to the emergency department with sudden onset chest pain and shortness of breath. Which of the following is the best next step in diagnosis?
subject: Medicine
explanation:
Solution
  • The clinical presentation is highly suggestive of pulmonary embolism.
  • The best diagnostic test is CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA), which directly visualizes thrombus within the pulmonary arteries.
  • It is fast, widely available, and highly sensitive.
  • D-dimer may be used as a screening tool in low-risk patients but is not reliable in post-operative patients because it can be elevated due to surgery.
  • Chest X-ray and MRI are not appropriate first-line investigations in this acute emergency.


Other Options:
Option A: This imaging may help rule out other causes of chest pain but is usually normal in pulmonary embolism.
Option B: This test is nonspecific in post-operative patients and cannot confirm pulmonary embolism.
Option D: This modality is not the investigation of choice for pulmonary embolism.

stem: A patient with a fracture of the tibia was managed with analgesics. After 2 days, the patient develops pain on dorsiflexion and loss of sensation in the first web space, but the distal pulses are still palpable. What is the next best management?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation:
Solution
  • The clinical picture suggests acute compartment syndrome. The hallmark is severe pain out of proportion to injury, especially on passive stretching, with early neurological changes such as loss of sensation in the first web space due to deep peroneal nerve compression.
  • Distal pulses may remain palpable because arterial flow is preserved until late stages.
  • The definitive and life-saving treatment is immediate fasciotomy to decompress the affected compartments and prevent irreversible ischemic necrosis.


Other Options:
Option B: Continuing analgesics does not address the underlying compartment pressure.
Option C: Observation would delay treatment and worsen muscle and nerve damage.
Option D: No alternative management is appropriate in this emergency.

stem: A 56-year-old female presents with chronic lower back pain. A lateral lumbar spine X-ray is provided. Based on the radiological findings, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation:
Solution
  • Spondylolisthesis is the forward displacement of one vertebra over another, most commonly at the lumbosacral junction (L4-L5 or L5-S1).
  • On lateral X-ray, it appears as anterior translation of the vertebral body relative to the one below.
  • Patients often present with chronic back pain, stiffness, and sometimes neurological symptoms.
  • It can result from defects in the pars interarticularis, degeneration, or trauma.


Other Options:
Option A: Spondylitis, such as ankylosing spondylitis, shows syndesmophytes and fusion rather than vertebral slippage.
Option C: Osteosarcoma usually shows aggressive bony destruction with periosteal reaction, not vertebral slipping.
Option D: A vertebral fracture would show loss of vertebral height or cortical break, not forward slippage.

stem: A patient presents with a lesion over the middle finger with surrounding redness on the dorsal aspect of the hand. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Surgery
explanation:
Solution
  • The clinical scenario describes infective tenosynovitis, which involves inflammation and infection of the tendon sheath.
  • It typically presents with localized swelling, pain, redness, and limitation of finger movements.
  • The infection can spread rapidly along the tendon sheath, making early diagnosis and treatment with antibiotics and sometimes surgical drainage essential.


Other Options:
Option A: This condition involves necrosis with putrefaction in ischemic tissue, not tendon sheath infection.
Option B: This condition results from chronic ischemia and shows mummified tissue, not acute inflammation.
Option C: This disease is a vasospastic disorder causing triphasic color changes, not localized finger swelling.

stem: A 45-year-old male presents with severe pain and swelling in the knee. Synovial fluid aspiration shows rhomboid-shaped crystals with positive birefringence under polarized light. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Rheumatology
explanation:
Solution
  • Pseudogout is caused by deposition of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate (CPPD) crystals in joints.
  • The characteristic feature of joint aspiration is rhomboid-shaped crystals that show positive birefringence under polarized light.
  • It typically affects large joints such as the knee and presents with acute episodes of pain, swelling, and restricted motion.
  • Radiographs may show chondrocalcinosis due to crystal deposition in cartilage.


Other Options:
Option B: Gout is caused by monosodium urate crystals, which are needle-shaped and show negative birefringence.
Option C: Osteoarthritis produces cartilage degeneration and osteophytes, but not crystals.
Option D: Septic arthritis produces purulent aspirate containing organisms, not crystal deposits.

stem: A 65-year-old male patient presented with lytic and sclerotic bone lesions on hip and pelvis X-rays and hat size seems to have increased over the past few years. Peripheral extremities were warm to touch. Lab investigations showed elevated ALP with other tests normal. What is the clinical diagnosis?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation:
Solution
  • Paget's disease of bone is characterized by a combination of lytic and sclerotic lesions due to disorganized bone remodeling.
  • A classic clinical feature is increase in hat size from skull involvement. Bones may feel warm due to hypervascularity.
  • Laboratory findings typically show elevated alkaline phosphatase with normal calcium and phosphate.
  • Complications include fractures, secondary osteoarthritis, and rarely osteosarcoma.


Other Options:
Option A: Osteomalacia shows diffuse bone pain and low vitamin D with hypocalcemia.
Option B: Osteoporosis presents with low bone density and fragility fractures, not warm bones or ALP elevation.
Option D: Hyperparathyroidism causes subperiosteal bone resorption and brown tumors, not mixed lytic-sclerotic lesions.

stem: A patient visited with pain in shoulder after hyperextension exercise. On examination a long head of triceps was found avulsed. What is the site of avulsion of triceps?
subject: Anatomy
explanation:
Solution
  • The long head of the triceps arises from the infraglenoid tubercle of the scapula.
  • Hyperextension or sudden forceful contraction can cause avulsion at this site.
  • Knowledge of anatomical origin is important in both trauma and surgical repair.


Other Options:
Option A: The shaft of the humerus is not the site of triceps attachment.
Option B: The supraglenoid tubercle gives origin to the long head of biceps, not triceps.
Option D: The deltoid tuberosity is the insertion site of the deltoid muscle, not triceps.

stem: In meralgia paraesthetica, the root value of the nerve compressed will be?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation:
Solution
  • Meralgia paraesthetica results from compression of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, which is derived from the L2-L3 roots.
  • It presents with burning pain, tingling, and numbness over the anterolateral thigh without motor involvement.
  • Risk factors include obesity, tight belts, and prolonged standing.


Other Options:
Option A: L4-L5 root lesions cause motor weakness in dorsiflexion, not isolated sensory thigh symptoms.
Option C: L1-L2 roots supply the iliohypogastric and ilioinguinal nerves, not the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve.
Option D: L3-L4 roots are involved in femoral nerve supply affecting quadriceps, not isolated thigh paresthesia.

stem: A male patient with femur fracture post road traffic accident, 2 days later presented with shortness of breath, rash, and confusion. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Orthopedics
explanation:
Solution
  • Fat embolism syndrome is a serious complication of long bone fractures, especially the femur.
  • Fat droplets from the bone marrow enter the circulation and lodge in pulmonary and cerebral microvasculature.
  • The classic triad is respiratory distress, petechial rash, and neurological symptoms (confusion, drowsiness, seizures) occurring 24-72 hours after trauma.
  • Diagnosis is clinical and management is supportive with oxygen and ventilation.


Other Options:
Option A: Sepsis may cause fever and shock but does not present with petechial rash in this context.
Option B: Pulmonary embolism causes sudden dyspnea but lacks petechial rash and neurological signs.
Option D: ARDS causes hypoxemia but without the rash and neurological involvement characteristic of fat embolism.

stem: An HIV-positive mother with a viral load of 1200 copies/mL delivers a full-term baby. What is the most appropriate antiretroviral prophylaxis for the newborn?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • Newborns of HIV-positive mothers with maternal viral load >1000 copies/mL require dual antiretroviral prophylaxis.
  • The standard regimen is nevirapine + zidovudine for 6-12 weeks, which reduces the risk of vertical transmission during delivery.
  • Monotherapy is insufficient at this viral load.
  • Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended along with continued maternal ART to minimize transmission risk.


Other Options:
Option A: Nevirapine alone is reserved for low-risk infants (maternal viral load <1000).
Option C: Prolonged nevirapine monotherapy is not recommended at this viral load.
Option D: Zidovudine alone is insufficient as prophylaxis in high-risk infants.

stem: Complementary feeding is initiated in a 7-month-old infant using the "katori method." What is the recommended amount and frequency of feeds in 24 hours?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • At 6-8 months, complementary feeding is introduced along with breastfeeding. The "katori method" uses a small bowl as a measure.
  • The recommendation is 1/2-1 katori per feed, 6 times a day, to ensure adequate calorie intake and nutritional diversity.
  • Frequent feeding compensates for small stomach capacity in infants.


Other Options:
Option A/B: Lower frequency feeding is inadequate for energy needs.
Option D: Eight times a day is excessive and unnecessary.

stem:
A 5-year-old child with CKD presents with bowing of legs. Lab findings:
Serum Calcium: 9.1 mg/dL (normal)
Serum Phosphate: 6.9 mg/dL (elevated)
ALP: Elevated
Vitamin D (25-OH): Low
What is the next best step in management?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • The child has renal rickets (CKD-related bone disease) with elevated phosphate, low vitamin D, and high ALP.
  • The treatment is oral calcium + vitamin D supplementation, which corrects the metabolic abnormalities and promotes bone mineralization.
  • Phosphate binders may also be required but the first-line approach is combined calcium and vitamin D.


Other Options:
Option A: Calcium alone will not correct vitamin D deficiency.
Option B: Phosphate binders are adjunctive, not primary in this setting.
Option D: Growth hormone is used for growth failure but does not address metabolic bone disease.

stem: A tall adolescent boy with lens subluxation, long limbs, and arachnodactyly is suspected to have a genetic disorder. Which gene mutation is most likely responsible?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • The described features are classic for Marfan syndrome: lens subluxation, arachnodactyly, tall stature, and long limbs.
  • It is caused by a mutation in the fibrillin-1 (FBN1) gene, leading to defective connective tissue integrity.
  • Patients are at risk for aortic root aneurysm and dissection, which are the most life-threatening complications.


Other Options:
Option B: Collagen type I mutation causes osteogenesis imperfecta with brittle bones and blue sclera.
Option C: Elastin gene mutation leads to Williams syndrome.
Option D: Dystrophin gene mutation causes Duchenne muscular dystrophy.

stem: A child suddenly develops severe coughing and respiratory distress after accidentally aspirating a peanut. What is the first-line immediate management?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • In children above 1 year with sudden airway obstruction due to foreign body aspiration, the first-line management is the Heimlich maneuver (abdominal thrusts).
  • This generates high intrathoracic pressure to expel the foreign body.
  • If unsuccessful, back blows and chest thrusts may be attempted, followed by advanced airway measures.
  • Immediate recognition and action are lifesaving.


Other Options:
Option B: Back slaps and chest thrusts are recommended for infants under 1 year.
Option C: Tracheostomy is a last resort if noninvasive methods fail.
Option D: Bronchodilators are not effective in mechanical obstruction.

stem: A 2-year-old child presents with a generalized seizure following high-grade fever. What is the first-line drug for seizure control in this acute febrile state?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • In febrile seizures, the first-line drug for acute control is diazepam, which is a fast-acting benzodiazepine.
  • It can be given intravenously or rectally in emergency settings.
  • Other antiepileptics like valproate or fosphenytoin are not required for simple febrile seizures.
  • Long-term antiepileptic prophylaxis is generally not indicated unless seizures are recurrent and complex.


Other Options:
Option B: Valproate is used for epilepsy prophylaxis, not acute febrile seizures.
Option C: Fosphenytoin is used for status epilepticus, not first-line in febrile seizures.
Option D: Antibiotics are irrelevant unless there is a bacterial infection causing fever.

stem: A 6-month-old infant has recurrent infections and failure to thrive. Lab tests show adenosine deaminase (ADA) deficiency. What is the most probable diagnosis?
subject: Pediatrics
explanation:
Solution
  • SCID (Severe Combined Immunodeficiency) is a life-threatening immunodeficiency characterized by impaired T and B cell function.
  • ADA deficiency is a classic cause that leads to accumulation of toxic metabolites that destroy lymphocytes.
  • Infants present with recurrent severe infections, failure to thrive, and chronic diarrhea.
  • Without treatment, it is usually fatal in early childhood. The definitive management is hematopoietic stem cell transplantation.


Other Options:
Option B: Hypogammaglobulinemia involves reduced immunoglobulins but does not cause ADA deficiency.
Option C: DiGeorge syndrome results from thymic aplasia and presents with hypocalcemia and congenital heart disease.
Option D: Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome is an X-linked disorder with thrombocytopenia, eczema, and recurrent infections, but no ADA deficiency.

stem: A junior intern is performing a procedure during the third stage of labor to deliver the placenta. Which procedure is being performed?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Controlled cord traction (CCT) is a key component of Active Management of the Third Stage of Labor (AMTSL).
  • After uterotonic administration, gentle traction is applied on the umbilical cord while counter-pressure is applied on the uterus.
  • Purpose: early and safe delivery of the placenta and to reduce risk of postpartum hemorrhage (PPH).
  • Recommended by WHO and standard in obstetric practice.
  • If not done properly, it may cause uterine inversion.


Other Options:
Option A: Uterine Massage is performed after delivery of the placenta to maintain uterine contraction, not to expel it.
Option C: Balloon Tamponade is used to control ongoing atonic PPH, not to deliver the placenta.
Option D: Evacuation of clot is a step in managing secondary PPH or retained products, not a routine part of placental delivery.

stem: A 28-year-old woman develops postpartum hemorrhage immediately after vaginal delivery. Which procedure can be performed in the labor room as an initial management step?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Balloon tamponade (Bakri balloon, condom catheter, etc) is a fertility-preserving, minimally invasive method to control PPH.
  • Inserted into the uterine cavity inflated provides pressure to stop bleeding.
  • Can be done in the labor room when medical management (oxytocin, ergometrine, misoprostol) fails.
  • Quick, effective and avoids immediate laparotomy.


Other Options:
Option A: Uterine artery embolization requires interventional radiology and is not feasible in an acute labor room setting.
Option B: B-Lynch suture is a surgical compression suture applied during laparotomy, not in the labor room.
Option D: Direct venous thrombectomy is not related to the management of postpartum hemorrhage.

stem: In a standard mediolateral episiotomy repair, what is the correct order of suturing?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • The correct repair sequence is:
  • 1. Vaginal mucosa (continuous)
  • 2. Muscle layer (interrupted)
  • 3. Skin (subcuticular or interrupted)
  • This restores anatomy and ensures hemostasis, healing and prevention of fistula/rectocele.


Other Options:
Option B: Skin first would leave deeper layers open, causing poor healing.
Option C: Muscle first without closing mucosa predisposes to bleeding and infection.
Option D: Vaginal mucosa → Skin → Muscle is anatomically incorrect and would result in poor tissue alignment.

stem: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with blood-stained vaginal discharge and a BP of 170/110 mmHg. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Reassure as normal due to low estrogen
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) is never normal and must be evaluated for carcinoma.
  • Work-up includes TVS for endometrial thickness followed by endometrial biopsy.
  • Hypertension here is incidental, not the priority.
  • Early evaluation improves survival in endometrial cancer.


Other Options:
Option A: Reassurance is unsafe because PMB is never physiological.
Option B: Cardiology referral addresses hypertension but ignores the primary risk of endometrial carcinoma.
Option D: Hormonal therapy should never be initiated before ruling out malignancy.

stem: A 46-year-old woman presents with abnormal uterine bleeding. TVS shows endometrial thickness of 14 mm. What is the next step?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Endometrial biopsy is mandatory when the endometrium is >12 mm in perimenopausal/postmenopausal women.
  • Confirms/rules out endometrial hyperplasia or carcinoma.
  • Hysterectomy or therapy should not be done without diagnosis.


Other Options:
Option A: Hysterectomy should only be performed once malignancy or precancer is proven, not as the first step.
Option C: Wait and watch is unsafe with a thickened endometrium.
Option D: Hormonal therapy cannot be given without histopathological confirmation.

stem: A 38-year-old multiparous woman, delivered twice previously by an untrained birth attendant, presents with uterine prolapse (image shown). Weakness of which ligament is primarily responsible for this condition?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Uterine prolapse occurs due to weakness of transverse cervical ligaments (Cardinal ligaments) which support the cervix and uterus.
  • Damage during childbirth weakens these supports descent of the uterus.
  • Risk factors: multiparity, poor obstetric care, chronic cough, heavy lifting.


Other Options:
Option A: Sacrospinous ligament is not a primary uterine support; it is used surgically for suspension procedures.
Option C: Round ligament maintains the anteverted position of the uterus but does not provide significant pelvic floor support.
Option D: Broad ligament is a peritoneal fold and does not act as a supportive structure.

stem: A 38-week pregnant woman in active labor with vertex at -1 station develops continuous suprapubic pain between contractions, fetal bradycardia and maternal tachycardia. What is the best next management?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Features: Suprapubic pain, fetal bradycardia, maternal tachycardia are classic for uterine rupture.
  • Emergency cesarean section is immediate management.
  • Delay increases risk of maternal shock and perinatal death.


Other Options:
Option A: Oxytocin augmentation would worsen uterine rupture by increasing contractions.
Option B: Amniotomy does not address the emergency and may aggravate the situation.
Option D: Forceps delivery is unsafe because the problem is uterine rupture, not obstructed second stage.

stem: According to the Pritchard regimen for eclampsia, the total IV + IM loading dose of magnesium sulfate is:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Pritchard regimen is the most widely used protocol for magnesium sulfate therapy in eclampsia.
  • Loading dose: 4 g IV slowly over 5 minutes + 10 g IM (5 g each buttock).
  • Total loading = 14 g.
  • Maintenance: 5 g IM every 4 hours for 24 hours after last convulsion/delivery.
  • Magnesium sulfate prevents recurrent seizures and protects maternal CNS without depressing the fetus.


Other Options:
Option B: 10 g refers only to the intramuscular component of the loading dose and is incomplete.
Option C: 4 g represents only the intravenous portion of the loading dose.
Option D: 6 g is the loading dose used in the Zuspan regimen, not the Pritchard regimen.

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with amenorrhea. Labs show elevated FSH and LH, with AMH <0.05 ng/mL. What is the diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) = amenorrhea before age 40 with hypergonadotropic hypogonadism.
  • Labs: ↑ FSH, ↑ LH, very low AMH (reflecting depleted ovarian reserve).
  • Causes include idiopathic, genetic (Turner syndrome, Fragile X premutation), autoimmune or iatrogenic.
  • Clinical presentation: amenorrhea, infertility, hypoestrogenic symptoms (hot flushes, vaginal dryness).
  • Different from menopause as ovarian activity may fluctuate.


Other Options:
Option A: Polycystic ovarian syndrome is characterized by normal/slightly elevated LH, low/normal FSH and elevated AMH, which is opposite to this case.
Option C: Premature menopause due to chemo is a cause of primary ovarian insufficiency but does not represent the diagnosis itself.
Option D: Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism shows low FSH and LH due to hypothalamic or pituitary dysfunction, which is not seen here.

stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with pelvic pain and heaviness for the past few months. MRI of the pelvis shows an enlarged uterus with multiple, well-defined, hypointense lesions on T2-weighted images. What is the most likely diagnosis?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Uterine fibroids (leiomyomas) are benign smooth muscle tumors of the uterus.
  • MRI shows well-circumscribed hypointense masses on T2 (due to compact smooth muscle + collagen).
  • Symptoms: pelvic pain, heaviness, menorrhagia, infertility.
  • Complications: anemia, recurrent pregnancy loss, pressure effects.
  • MRI is the gold standard for mapping fibroid number, size and location.


Other Options:
Option A: Adenomyosis presents with a diffusely enlarged, bulky uterus and poorly defined areas on imaging, not well-circumscribed lesions.
Option C: Endometrial carcinoma typically presents with postmenopausal bleeding, not with multiple intramural lesions.
Option D: Pelvic inflammatory disease produces tubo-ovarian abscesses or adnexal masses but does not cause discrete myometrial lesions.

stem: During breech delivery, the fetus's arm is extended above the head. Which maneuver is used to deliver the arms?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Lovset's maneuver is specifically designed for delivery of extended arms during breech presentation.
  • The fetal body is rotated to bring one shoulder anterior under the symphysis pubis, then the arm is swept down across the chest. The same is repeated in the opposite direction for the other arm.
