  • This avoids arm fracture, brachial plexus injury and cervical spine trauma.
  • Often combined with subsequent maneuvers for head delivery (e.g. MSV maneuver).
  • It is a crucial step in safe vaginal breech management.


Other Options:
Option A: Ritgen's maneuver is used to control the delivery of the fetal head in vertex presentation, not for breech arms.
Option C: Mauriceau-Smellie-Veit maneuver is used for delivery of the after-coming head in breech, not the arms.
Option D: Pinard maneuver is employed to deliver extended fetal legs in breech, not arms.

stem: Drug of choice for prevention of venous thromboembolism after cesarean section is:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin) is the preferred prophylactic agent for preventing post-cesarean VTE.
  • Safe in pregnancy and puerperium because it does not cross placenta or appear in breast milk.
  • Advantages: predictable pharmacokinetics, less monitoring than unfractionated heparin, lower risk of HIT/osteoporosis.
  • Given as subcutaneous injection, dose adjusted by risk category.
  • Oral anticoagulants (warfarin, NOACs) are avoided in pregnancy.


Other Options:
Option A: Aspirin acts as an antiplatelet agent and is not effective enough for VTE prophylaxis in the obstetric setting.
Option B: Clopidogrel is also an antiplatelet drug and is not recommended for prevention of thromboembolism in cesarean patients.
Option D: Warfarin is contraindicated in pregnancy and puerperium because it crosses the placenta and is teratogenic; it is also unsafe during breastfeeding.

stem: What is the correct recommended sequence of maneuvers for managing shoulder dystocia?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • McRoberts maneuver (maternal hips hyperflexed onto abdomen) is always first-line → straightens sacrum and enlarges pelvic outlet.
  • If unsuccessful, Rubin maneuver (rotating fetal shoulders) is attempted.
  • Next option: Gaskin maneuver (mother on all fours).
  • Zavanelli maneuver (cephalic replacement and cesarean) is the last resort when all else fails.
  • Prompt execution prevents brachial plexus injury, hypoxia, neonatal death.


Other Options:
Option A: Rubin → McRoberts → Gaskin → Zavanelli is incorrect because McRoberts is always the first-line maneuver.
Option C: Zavanelli → Gaskin → Rubin → McRoberts is unrealistic as Zavanelli is never performed before simpler maneuvers.
Option D: McRoberts → Zavanelli → Rubin → Gaskin is incorrect because Zavanelli is not attempted immediately after McRoberts.

stem: A 26-year-old woman presents with amenorrhea, breast enlargement, linea nigra and bluish discoloration of the vaginal mucosa (Chadwick's sign). According to pregnancy signs classification, these findings are considered:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Probable signs of pregnancy are objective changes seen by the examiner but not confirmatory.
  • Examples: Chadwick's sign (bluish vagina), Goodell's sign (soft cervix), uterine enlargement, positive pregnancy test.
  • They strongly suggest pregnancy but may occur in other conditions (e.g. pelvic congestion, tumors).
  • Confirmatory signs require definite evidence (ultrasound fetal cardiac activity, fetal heart sounds).
  • Presumptive signs are subjective (amenorrhea, nausea, breast changes felt by the patient).


Other Options:
Option A: Confirmatory signs are not a standard classification term.
Option C: Presumptive signs are subjective symptoms reported by the patient, not the examiner's findings.
Option D: Diagnostic signs include fetal heart sounds, ultrasound visualization or palpation of fetal parts, not Chadwick's sign.

stem: A pregnant woman in her first trimester undergoes routine antenatal screening and is found to have positive IgG antibodies for toxoplasmosis. She gives a history of infection 3-5 years ago. What is the most appropriate management?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Positive IgG with negative IgM indicates past infection, not active disease.
  • Past infection confers immunity → no risk of congenital toxoplasmosis in this pregnancy.
  • No treatment or intervention is required; simple reassurance is enough.
  • Spiramycin or pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine are used in recent or acute infections.
  • Unnecessary treatment may harm the mother/fetus.


Other Options:
Option A: Spiramycin is used to prevent transplacental transmission in acute maternal infection.
Option B: Pyrimethamine-sulfadiazine is reserved for confirmed fetal infection, not past exposure.
Option C: Amniocentesis for fetal PCR is indicated if maternal infection occurs during pregnancy, not with past immunity.

stem: Normally, the umbilical cord contains how many blood vessels?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Normal umbilical cord contains 2 umbilical arteries + 1 umbilical vein (total 3 vessels).
  • Arteries carry deoxygenated blood from the fetus to the placenta, while the vein carries oxygenated blood to the fetus.
  • Single umbilical artery (SUA) is a common anomaly, associated with renal, cardiac malformations and IUGR.
  • Examination of cord vessels is part of routine neonatal check.
  • Important clue in prenatal ultrasound when assessing anomalies.


Other Options:
Option A: represents a single artery and single vein, which is abnormal.
Option C: is not physiologically possible.
Option D: with four vessels is also abnormal and extremely rare.

stem: CA-125 levels: 1025, with ascites and unilateral ovarian mass with papillary projections. Diagnosis:
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Serous cystadenocarcinoma is the most common type of malignant ovarian tumor.
  • Typical features: postmenopausal age, ascites, papillary excrescences, very high CA-125.
  • Spread commonly involves peritoneal surfaces → ascites, omental caking.
  • Poor prognosis due to late presentation.
  • Requires staging laparotomy and chemotherapy.


Other Options:
Option A: Teratoma is usually benign and not associated with elevated CA-125.
Option C: Yolk sac tumor typically presents in younger women and is associated with elevated AFP, not CA-125.
Option D: Dysgerminoma elevates LDH, not CA-125.

stem: Hormone profile during Day 21-25 of menstrual cycle?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Days 21-25 correspond to the mid-luteal phase.
  • Progesterone is high (secreted by corpus luteum).
  • FSH and LH are suppressed via negative feedback on hypothalamus-pituitary.
  • Estrogen remains moderate, maintaining endometrial proliferation.
  • Progesterone dominance ensures secretory changes in endometrium for implantation.


Other Options:
Option B: High progesterone, High FSH/LH is incorrect since gonadotropins are suppressed.
Option C: Low progesterone, High LH/FSH reflects an anovulatory cycle.
Option D: Low progesterone, Low LH/FSH suggests hypothalamic dysfunction, not a normal luteal phase.

stem: 36 weeks, 8 cm cervical dilation with cord prolapse. What will you do next?
options: Push up presenting part & retrograde filling of bladder
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Umbilical cord prolapse is an emergency causing cord compression and fetal hypoxia.
  • Immediate step: push the presenting part up manually to relieve compression.
  • Retrograde bladder filling with saline helps elevate the presenting part until cesarean delivery.
  • Definitive management = emergency cesarean section.
  • Delay leads to severe fetal hypoxia and death.


Other Options:
Option A is incorrect; knee-chest or Trendelenburg position can actually help relieve cord compression.
Option C: Wait for progress is unsafe because immediate intervention is required.
Option D: None is inappropriate as urgent action is needed.

stem: A 45-year-old woman is diagnosed with cervical cancer. Imaging shows hydronephrosis due to tumor extension causing ureteral obstruction but no distant metastasis. According to the FIGO staging, what is the correct stage of this cancer?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • FIGO IIIB = tumor extending to the pelvic wall and/or causing hydronephrosis or non-functioning kidney.
  • In this case, hydronephrosis indicates ureteric obstruction by tumor infiltration.
  • No distant metastasis excludes Stage IV.
  • Accurate staging is crucial for prognosis and treatment (usually chemoradiation).


Other Options:
Option A: Stage IIB involves parametrial invasion but no hydronephrosis.
Option B: Stage IIIA indicates tumor involving the lower third of the vagina without pelvic wall involvement.
Option D: Stage IVA involves spread to adjacent pelvic organs such as bladder or rectum, not seen here.

stem: A 28-year-old female with irregular cycles, now amenorrheic for 2 months. UPT is positive. Best way to date the pregnancy?
subject: Obstetrics and Gynecology
explanation:
Solution
  • Crown-rump length (CRL) is the most accurate method for dating pregnancy in the first trimester (6-12 weeks).
  • Accuracy: ±3-5 days.
  • Mean sac diameter is used before the embryo is visible (<6 weeks).
  • Biparietal diameter (BPD) is reliable only after 12 weeks.
  • Abdominal circumference is for growth assessment in later trimesters, not dating.


Other Options:
Option A: Mean sac diameter is used very early (before embryo visualization) but is less accurate once CRL can be measured.
Option C: Biparietal diameter is accurate in the second trimester, not in the first.
Option D: Abdominal circumference is used for fetal growth, not for dating.
